summary: USER_FLOWS.md covers the install/ingest/retrieve/sync/recovery spine well (F1-F12) and its requirement→flow table is internally consistent for PB-1..PB-11. But the coverage table is built against the abstracted REQUIREMENTS extraction, not the PRD's full surface, and several PRD-implied user/lifecycle flows are absent or collapsed. The biggest gaps: (1) PRD §9.2 defines FIVE action modes (read-only, Draft, Confirmed-Action, Approved-Workflow, Policy-Automation) but only Mode 4 maps to a flow (F11) — Draft (Mode 2), single Confirmed-Action (Mode 3), and Policy-Automation (Mode 5) have no flow even though they are listed as standalone-relevant capabilities and PB-8 MUST-support all five; (2) PRD §11.1 lists six drawer modes (Ask/Plan/Review/Decisions/Memory/Actions) but only Ask and Actions have flows — Plan/Review/Decisions/Memory are unflowed; (3) first-run/empty-state (machine set up but no projects yet) and the multi-project scope-selection/switching interaction are not flows; (4) the onboarding "doc-completeness nudge → accept/decline /layer-docs" interaction and per-project session-consent REVOCATION/purge are not flows; (5) user-initiated provider/policy change (the trigger that fires the F9 blue-green re-embed) has no flow. Most are P1/P2-tier or standalone-thin, so the load-bearing ones are the action-mode collapse (contract-shaping for PB-8) and the consent-revocation gap (privacy invariant). Many are correctly deferrable but should be explicitly named-and-deferred rather than silently absent.

findings.finding: PRD §9.2 defines five Action Modes — Mode 1 Read-only, Mode 2 Draft, Mode 3 Confirmed Action (single), Mode 4 Approved Workflow (bundle), Mode 5 Policy Automation — and PB-8 states the system MUST support all five. USER_FLOWS only models F11 (Action plan → Gateway), which is the Mode-4 bundled-workflow case, and tags it [deferred — P2]. There is no flow for Mode 2 (Draft: draft a PR description / Linear issue / Claude prompt / plan update — explicitly listed as standalone-meaningful in PRD §9.2), no flow for Mode 3 (a single confirmed action), and no flow for Mode 5 (policy-automation: auto-summarize sessions, auto-link commits, auto-mark tasks). The PRD §9.2 Mode-2 Draft examples produce content with NO mutation, so they are not even strictly integration-gated — yet they have no representation.
findings.evidence: PRD.md §9.2 Action Modes + §10 PB-8; USER_FLOWS.md F11 (only action-plan flow, tagged deferred-P2) + coverage line 'PB-8 → F11'
findings.proposed_fix: Add explicit flows (or sub-flows) for Mode 2 (Draft — standalone, no mutation, just produces drafted content + evidence), Mode 3 (single Confirmed Action via HostPort allowlist/Gateway), and Mode 5 (Policy Automation — user-defined policy fires a risk-0/low action). At minimum, split F11 to show the Mode-3 vs Mode-4 difference and state which modes are standalone vs integrated-only. PB-8 MUST-supports all five, so the finalized ARCHITECTURE must say where each lives.

findings.finding: PRD §11.1 lists six embedded drawer modes — Ask, Plan, Review, Decisions, Memory, Actions — confirmed as DECIDED in MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE §6. USER_FLOWS covers Ask (F3/F4) and Actions (F11) but has NO flow for Plan, Review, Decisions, or Memory. 'Plan' (plan-task awareness / next-task), 'Review' (PR/diff explanation), 'Decisions' (decision-ledger lookup), and 'Memory' (session/episode browsing, since-you-left) are distinct user-facing read/reason surfaces, not just Ask synonyms. Even if all four are integration/P2-deferred, the absence is silent — the coverage table claims 'No in-scope requirement is without a flow' without acknowledging these modes.
findings.evidence: PRD.md §11.1 Drawer Modes; MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md §6 (DECIDED drawer modes); USER_FLOWS.md (only F3/F4 Ask, F11 Actions)
findings.proposed_fix: Either add a flow per non-Ask drawer mode (Plan/Review/Decisions/Memory) showing the retrieval+scope+evidence path, or add one explicit 'drawer-mode read surfaces' deferred flow that enumerates them and marks them [P2, integrated]. Don't leave them implicit under 'ask'.

findings.finding: No first-run / empty-state flow. After Flow 1 (setup) but before any Flow 2 (add), the user has a working install with zero projects. USERS.md describes the workflow as 'setup once → add repos → ask', and the Tauri desktop UI (D-17) is a primary surface, but there is no flow for what the desktop app / CLI presents when the registry is empty, or the very first 'add → first grounded answer' guided onboarding (which the PRD success metric 'Time to first grounded answer after project add' implies is a tracked journey).
findings.evidence: PRD.md §15 Freshness metric 'Time to first grounded answer after project add'; USERS.md §Workflow; D-17/§11 desktop UI; USER_FLOWS.md has F1 setup and F2 add but no first-run/empty-state
findings.proposed_fix: Add a short first-run flow (empty registry → desktop/CLI prompts 'add your first repo' → F2 → first ask) OR fold an explicit empty-state + first-answer onboarding note into F1/F2 so the 'time to first grounded answer' metric has a journey behind it.

findings.finding: Session-consent REVOCATION / per-project session-data purge is not a flow. PB-11 + PB-5 make session ingestion opt-in and 'stricter than docs/code'; Flow 7 covers the consent GATE and the no-consent skip, but there is no flow for a user who previously consented and now wants to revoke consent and purge already-embedded episode cards / session-derived data for a project. THREAT_MODEL/privacy posture (D-15, redaction) treats transcripts as the most sensitive input, so the ability to withdraw and erase is a privacy-baseline expectation for a production-grade local-first tool. F12 (uninstall) only covers whole-machine teardown, not per-project session-consent reversal.
findings.evidence: PRD.md PB-5/PB-11 (opt-in, stricter); USER_FLOWS.md F7 (consent gate only, no revocation path); F12 (machine-level uninstall only)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a flow (or extend F7/F12) for 'revoke session consent for project X' → stop ingestion → tombstone + purge all session-derived embeddings/episode cards for that project → confirm. Mirror it for revoking cloud-provider consent. Confirm with owner whether revocation purges or just quarantines.

findings.finding: User-initiated provider/policy change has no flow — only the system-side consequence does. Flow 9 (blue-green re-embed) is triggered by 'embedding model/dim change', and D-23 says providers are chosen per-project in policy.yaml and 'switching a provider = blue-green re-embed'. But there is no flow for the USER action that initiates this: editing policy.yaml / choosing a different embedding or privacy posture for an existing project, and the preview/consent before a potentially large re-embed kicks off. The trigger side of F9 is unowned.
findings.evidence: PRD.md §13 Open Q / D-23 (provider switch = blue-green re-embed); USER_FLOWS.md F9 (triggered by model/dim change, no user-initiated entry); USERS.md open Q on default privacy posture/embedding model
findings.proposed_fix: Add a short 'change project provider/privacy posture' flow: user edits policy.yaml → system estimates re-embed cost → preview + consent → F9 blue-green re-embed. Ties the user trigger to the existing system flow.

findings.finding: The onboarding 'doc-completeness nudge → accept/decline /layer-docs' interaction is described as a capability but not modeled as a user flow. R-PARTIAL (ONBOARDING §R-PARTIAL) says add must 'suggest running /layer-docs (or offer a non-interactive best-effort pass) but don't block' and 'surface a doc-completeness signal'. F2 mentions 'nudge /layer-docs but don't block' and 'surface a doc completeness signal' in system-responsibilities, but the user-facing accept/decline loop (and the supplemental-doc generation path for owned/gap areas, Flow-8-adjacent) is collapsed into a one-liner rather than a flow with its consent branch.
findings.evidence: ONBOARDING_AND_DOC_LIFECYCLE.md §R-PARTIAL + 'Gaps to fill' (doc-gap analysis → suggest layer-docs); USER_FLOWS.md F2 system-responsibility line; F8 (owned-doc refresh but for drift, not initial gap-fill)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a small sub-flow or explicit branch for the doc-completeness nudge: add detects thin docs → surfaces completeness signal → user accepts → supplemental layer-docs generation (namespaced, don't-clobber) → re-ingest; or declines → proceed code-only. Distinguish initial gap-fill from F8 drift-refresh.

findings.finding: P3 'Since-you-left summaries' and 'cross-project impact spike' (PRD §14 Roadmap P3) have no flows and are not named as deferred in USER_FLOWS. Since-you-left is a distinct user-facing read journey (open project → 'here's what happened while you were away' from episode cards + commits + PRs), not the same as ad-hoc ask (F3). It's correctly P3-tier, but the coverage table's blanket 'no in-scope requirement is without a flow' should explicitly carve these out as deferred rather than omit them.
findings.evidence: PRD.md §14 Roadmap P3 (Since-you-left summaries, Cross-project impact spike); USER_FLOWS.md (no flow, not listed in coverage table)
findings.proposed_fix: Add 'Since-you-left' and 'cross-project impact' as explicitly [deferred — P3] flow stubs (one line each) so the deferral is intentional and traceable, not silent.

findings.finding: Scope selection / multi-project switching as an interactive user action is implied but not flowed. PRD §11.2 + MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE §6 define a 7-chip scope selector (project/session/file/diff/PR/plan-task/agent-team) plus standalone supersets (portfolio/worktree/selection), and F-LCR routing depends on resolved scope. F3 step 1 says 'resolve scope' and F5 is the portfolio fan-out, but there is no flow describing the user-facing act of switching active project / changing scope chips and how that re-routes retrieval (especially the standalone desktop project-switcher). Unclear whether the owner considers this a UI-detail (out of flow scope) or a missing flow.
findings.evidence: PRD.md §11.2 Scope Selector; MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md §6 (7 chips + standalone supersets); USER_FLOWS.md F3 step 1 ('resolve scope'), F5 (portfolio) — no scope-switch interaction flow
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm with owner: should multi-project switching / scope-chip selection be a first-class flow (it shapes the standalone desktop UX and the retrieval-scope query parameter), or is it intentionally treated as UI detail under F3/F5? If the former, add a scope-selection flow.

summary: The draft names seven-plus state machines but only DATA_MODEL §State machines + DOMAIN_MODEL §State machines actually enumerate them, and every one is underspecified for a production-grade build: terminal states, failure/error states, back-transitions to the healthy state, and illegal-transition handling are almost entirely absent. The Index-generation machine omits a build-failed state and leaves `swapped`/old-generation lifecycle ambiguous against the LanceDB GC contract. The Anchor machine has no recovery transitions and no deleted-target terminal. The Project machine has no error/failed state and conflates archived vs removed. The Worker machine omits crash + the single-writer-lock-conflict state that D-25 W3 makes load-bearing. Two machines the prompt explicitly names — the **EpisodeCard lifecycle** and the **provider blue-green swap states** — have NO state machine at all (only prose flows F7/F9). The **WorkflowInstance** 12 frozen states are listed (PRD §7.8) but their transitions are defined nowhere. Personalization Run / Plan / Plan Task / ActionPlan all carry a `status` field with zero enumerated states. These are baseline correctness/operability gaps under the no-timebox production posture, not deferrable polish.

findings.finding: The EpisodeCard lifecycle has NO state machine, despite being explicitly required by the audit scope and despite an opt-in/consent/redaction/quarantine flow that is security-load-bearing. DOMAIN_MODEL and DATA_MODEL enumerate state machines for Index/Anchor/Project/Worker/Doc but omit EpisodeCard entirely. Flow 7 (F7) describes the path as prose (consent gate → read → redact + exclude thinking → summarize → embed card → commit-link with confidence; quarantine on redaction-unsafe), but there is no state model with terminal states, no failure/abort transitions, and no idempotency state for re-ingesting the same session. Because redaction-unsafe → quarantine (SR-1, SR-3) and consent-revocation are both safety-critical, the absence of a state machine means the deletion/quarantine/consent-revoked terminal states and the 'never embed raw / never embed thinking' invariant have no state to anchor a test against (FR-14, PH-2).
findings.evidence: DOMAIN_MODEL.md:§State machines (lists Index/Anchor/Project/Worker/Doc; no EpisodeCard); USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 7; PRD.md:§7.4 + §15; DATA_MODEL.md:§Core domain objects (EpisodeCard schema present, no state field)
findings.proposed_fix: Add an EpisodeCard state machine: `no_consent → consent_granted → reading → redacting → (quarantined_unsafe terminal | redacted) → summarizing → embedded → linked`, with terminal states `consent_revoked` (purge embedded card + raw) and `superseded` (re-ingest of same session_id). Specify that `thinking`-excluded + raw-never-embedded are invariants checked at the `redacting→redacted` edge, and that quarantine is per-span (partial card still embeddable) per F7.

findings.finding: The provider blue-green swap is described only as a flow (F9) and a one-line note in DATA_MODEL Index-generation machine; there is no explicit swap-orchestration state machine, which the audit scope explicitly asks for. The Index-generation machine reads `building → active → (reembedding → swapped) | degraded | rebuilding`, but: (a) `swapped` appears to be a transient action, not a stable state — after an atomic swap the NEW generation should be `active` and the OLD generation needs its own retained/GC state; (b) there is no `validation` state between re-embed completion and the atomic swap (F9 step 2 says 'atomic swap query path' but nothing gates the swap on the new generation being correct/complete); (c) `dim mismatch → hard rebuild` vs `same-dim/new-model → re-embed` (DATA_MODEL invariant 3) are two different entry paths into the machine that aren't modeled as distinct transitions; (d) crash-mid-reembed is stated to keep the prior generation `active` (PH-4) but there is no `reembedding_failed`/`resumable` state to model the idempotent-delta-resume.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:§State machines (Index generation) + §Reproducibility invariants 2-3; USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 9; REQUIREMENTS.md:PH-4/PH-5; DECISIONS.md:D-14(5)
findings.proposed_fix: Define an explicit blue-green generation state machine with parallel old/new tracking: `active(G_old)` + `building(G_new) → reembedding(G_new) → validating(G_new) → swapping → active(G_new) + retired(G_old) → (G_old) gc_eligible → purged`. Add failure edges: `reembedding/validating failed → G_new discarded, G_old stays active`. Model dim-mismatch (hard rebuild, drop G_old after swap) vs same-dim re-embed (keep G_old for rollback) as distinct entry transitions. Tie `retired→gc_eligible→purged` to the `cleanup_old_versions()` / git-SHA-tag GC-exemption contract (D-25 W2) so rollback retention vs disk-bloat is a defined, testable window.

findings.finding: The Worker state machine `cold → warm (on-demand) → idle-evicted (LRU)` omits the two states the build's own contracts make load-bearing: (a) NO crash/failed state, even though Flow 6/Flow 10 promise 'worker crash → idempotent resume from the content-hash manifest' and 'cheap re-attach' — there is no `crashed`/`reattaching` state to anchor that recovery; (b) NO single-writer-lock state, even though D-25 W3 + invariant 7 make 'single-writer-per-index' a hard correctness rule and call optimistic-commit conflicts the reason the federation router is read-only. A worker that wants to write must acquire the single-writer lock; lock-held-by-another and lock-conflict are unmodeled states. Without these, the idempotent-resume and single-writer invariants have no state to test illegal transitions against.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:§State machines (Project line, worker sub-machine); USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 6 + Flow 10; DECISIONS.md:D-25 maintenance contract (3); DOMAIN_MODEL.md:invariant 7
findings.proposed_fix: Extend the Worker machine: `cold → warming → warm ⇄ writing(holds single-writer lease) → idle → idle_evicted(LRU)`, plus `crashed → reattaching → warm` (resume from content-hash manifest, idempotent) and `write_blocked` (single-writer lease held elsewhere → queue or fail-with-marker). Specify the lease/fencing semantics (a stale writer must be rejected, mirroring NexusOps fencing-token rule) and that the federation router NEVER enters `writing`.

findings.finding: The Anchor state machine `live → stale | moved | unknown` has no recovery transitions and no deleted-target terminal. Anchors are 'continuously revalidated' (D-6) and Flow 8 re-marks them on every sync, so a `stale` anchor whose doc gets refreshed, or a `moved` anchor that is re-resolved, MUST be able to return to `live` — but the machine is drawn as one-directional out of `live` with no edges back or between the non-live states. There is also no terminal state for when the target file/symbol is deleted (anchor can never become live again — distinct from `unknown` which means 'couldn't resolve'). This matters because the grounding gate (D-7, the north star) keys on `live`, so the exact set of states an anchor can re-enter `live` from is contract-critical for PR-1 (stale-anchor false-confidence = 0).
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:§State machines (Anchor); DECISIONS.md:D-6/D-7; USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 8; RISKS.md:PR-1
findings.proposed_fix: Make the Anchor machine bidirectional with an explicit revalidation transition from {stale, moved, unknown} → live (on successful re-resolve at a new SHA), moved → live (after target re-found + anchor rewritten), and add a terminal `orphaned`/`deleted` state (target file or symbol removed) distinct from `unknown` (transient resolution failure). State which states the grounding gate treats as servable-as-cited (only `live`) vs flag-only.

findings.finding: The Project state machine `added → indexing → ready ⇄ syncing → drift_detected → reindexing → archived/removed` has three gaps: (a) NO error/failed state — F2 says 'partial ingest → temp generation, no half-swapped index' and 'corrupt file → skip + log', and F10 says 'corrupt store → rebuild from source', but there is no `index_failed`/`degraded` project state to land in when initial ingest or a reindex fails; (b) `drift_detected` only flows forward to `reindexing` — there is no edge back to `ready` for drift that is acknowledged/dismissed without a reindex, and no edge for foreign-doc drift which is 'flag, never overwrite' (Flow 8 step 4) and therefore should NOT force `reindexing`; (c) `archived/removed` are conflated but are different terminals — archived keeps the registry row + stores (recoverable), removed purges (matches `uninstall`/F12 'preserve local data' option). Illegal-transition handling (e.g. querying a project still in `indexing`, or `add` on an already-`ready` project = re-add/upgrade per R-PARTIAL) is unspecified.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:§State machines (Project); USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 2 + Flow 8 + Flow 10 + Flow 12; ONBOARDING_AND_DOC_LIFECYCLE.md:R-PARTIAL
findings.proposed_fix: Add `index_failed`/`degraded` (initial ingest or reindex error → remediation/retry; queryable code-only if partial) and split terminals into `archived` (registry-retained, re-activatable) vs `removed` (purged). Add `drift_detected → ready` (drift dismissed / foreign-only drift annotated, no reindex). Specify illegal transitions: query-while-indexing returns a not-ready marker; re-`add` on `ready` is an idempotent upgrade (re-ingest delta), not a duplicate.

findings.finding: The 12 frozen WorkflowInstance states are LISTED (PRD §7.8: not_detected, pack_available, needs_personalization, personalization_in_progress, generated_review_required, active, ready_for_team_run, degraded, drift_detected, upgrade_available, archived*, detached*) but the TRANSITIONS between them are defined NOWHERE in the planning artifacts. DOMAIN_MODEL and DATA_MODEL both just say 'WorkflowInstance = the 12 frozen R-7 states' and point at the PRD. For a state set that is a FROZEN cross-product contract with NexusOps (mirrors `shared/src/status.rs`), the absence of the legal transition graph means the Brain's detection-heuristic → status mapping (PRD §8.2) could emit illegal status sequences the platform rejects. E.g. can `degraded` → `active` self-heal? Can `upgrade_available` coexist with `active`? Is `archived` reachable from any state or only `active`? Two terminals (`archived`, `detached`) are marked but their entry edges are unspecified.
findings.evidence: PRD.md:§7.8 + §8.2; DOMAIN_MODEL.md:§State machines (last line); DATA_MODEL.md:§Core domain objects (last bullet); MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:§3 (workflow_instance_id frozen)
findings.proposed_fix: Even though WorkflowInstance ingestion is P1/P2, freeze the transition graph now since the STATES are frozen: define which states are entry (not_detected), which are terminal (archived, detached), and the legal edges (e.g. needs_personalization → personalization_in_progress → generated_review_required → active → ready_for_team_run; active ↔ degraded; active → drift_detected → active; * → upgrade_available → personalization_in_progress). Flag any transition the Brain only DETECTS (heuristic) vs receives from the platform, and confirm with NexusOps whether the transition graph (not just the state set) is part of the frozen R-7 contract — this is a question-for-human.

findings.finding: The Doc lifecycle is presented as a classification + action table (owned → regenerate+replace; foreign → re-ingest, annotate; supplemental → namespaced), NOT as a state machine — yet the owned-doc refresh in Flow 8 is a genuine multi-step stateful workflow with a consent gate and a conflict terminal: propose → preview → consent → regenerate (don't-clobber/3-way-merge) → re-embed, OR merge-conflict → leave untouched + surface. None of these refresh states are enumerated. Because the refresh is the only place the StandaloneHost performs a bounded mutation on a user file (D-4, SR-4 allowlist-violation = 0), the proposed/consented/merged/conflict/rejected states are exactly where the audit + reversibility invariant must attach, and they're missing.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:§State machines (Doc lifecycle as classification); USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 8 steps 3-5; DECISIONS.md:D-4; RISKS.md:SR-4
findings.proposed_fix: Add an owned-doc-refresh state machine distinct from the ownership classification: `drift_flagged → refresh_proposed → (rejected terminal | consented) → regenerating → (merge_conflict terminal: doc untouched + surfaced | merged) → re-embedding → done`. Record the audit event + reversibility checkpoint at the `consented→regenerating` edge. Keep owned/foreign/supplemental as a separate classification attribute, not a lifecycle.

findings.finding: Several entities carry a `status` field in the PRD with ZERO enumerated states or transitions: Workflow Personalization Run (§7.9 `status`), Implementation Plan (§7.5 `status`), Plan Task (§7.6 `status`), and Action Plan (§7.10 `approval_mode` but the plan's own lifecycle status is implicit). Personalization Run in particular is a stateful process (infer → ask unresolved questions → generate diff → approvals → resulting manifest) that USER_FLOWS does not even cover (no flow for it; OQ OQ-... none), yet PRD Decision 4 + §8 make pack-vs-instance-vs-personalization-run a core distinction. Plan Task status drives the 'next unstarted backend task' selection in the marquee Action-Plan example (PRD §19) — undefined task states make 'unstarted/in-progress/in-review/done' ambiguous.
findings.evidence: PRD.md:§7.5/§7.6/§7.9/§7.10 + §8.1 + §19; USER_FLOWS.md (no personalization-run flow); OPEN_QUESTIONS.md:OQ-8 (cc-crew parsing) / OQ-11 (personalization replay)
findings.proposed_fix: Enumerate the status values for each: PlanTask `{unstarted, in_progress, in_review, done, blocked}` (and map to PR-open auto-transition in Mode 5 policy automation); ImplementationPlan `{parsed, partially_linked, fully_linked, stale}`; PersonalizationRun `{inferring, awaiting_answers, generating, awaiting_approval, applied, abandoned}` and add a USER_FLOW for it (it is currently flow-orphaned despite REQUIREMENTS claiming full coverage). Mark P1/P2 ones as spec-now-build-later, but they must be defined where the field exists.

findings.finding: No state machine governs illegal-transition / fail-closed handling as a cross-cutting rule, even though the production posture (D-1/D-18) makes error paths baseline. Each machine is drawn as a happy path with at most one degrade branch; none specifies what happens on an undefined transition (e.g. a sync event arriving for a project in `added` before `indexing` completes; a swap requested while a prior swap is `swapping`; an anchor revalidation racing a re-embed generation change). The CONSTRAINTS/THREAT_MODEL fail-closed posture (mirrored from NexusOps safety rule #5 'fail-closed on audit-write') is not reflected in any state-machine guard.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:§State machines (all happy-path); DECISIONS.md:D-1/D-18 (production posture); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§4.2 (laws, no transition guards); NexusOps/CLAUDE.md:safety rule 5 (fail-closed precedent)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a one-paragraph cross-cutting rule to DATA_MODEL §State machines: undefined transitions are rejected (fail-closed), logged to the ObservabilitySink, and leave the entity in its prior state; concurrent same-entity mutations are serialized by the single-writer lease (D2-03). Name the specific races to guard: sync-during-indexing, swap-during-swap, anchor-revalidation-during-generation-swap.

findings.finding: The CodeGraph external dependency has lifecycle behavior (F10: 'CodeGraph daemon down → 300s idle is normal; reconnect does catch-up sync; one-shot CLI fallback') but no connection-state model (connected → idle → disconnected → reconnecting → catch-up → connected; CLI-fallback degraded mode). Since store ② is read-only and external, this is lower stakes than the owned machines, but the retrieval pipeline's graph-tools-always-on assumption (D-13) silently depends on which CodeGraph state is active, and the schema-version gate (read gated on `schema_versions`) adds a `schema_mismatch → excluded` state that is mentioned (F5/PH-9) but not modeled.
findings.evidence: USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 10 (CodeGraph) + Flow 5; DECISIONS.md:D-13 (graph tools always-on) + D-12; REQUIREMENTS.md:PH-9
findings.proposed_fix: Add a brief CodeGraph connection-state note: `connected ⇄ idle(<300s) → disconnected → reconnecting → catchup → connected`, with `cli_fallback` and `schema_mismatch_excluded` as degraded branches that the retrieval router checks before claiming graph-tools-on. Optional given it is an external read-only dependency.

findings.finding: It is unclear whether the WorkflowInstance TRANSITION graph (not just the 12-state SET) is part of the frozen R-7 contract with NexusOps, or whether the Brain is free to define its own legal transitions for its detection heuristics. PRD §8.2 says the Brain-side detection states are 'heuristics, not the platform's status values' and map ONTO the 12 frozen states — but never says whether the platform also freezes the legal transition edges. This determines whether D2-06's transition graph is something the Brain authors unilaterally or must co-design with NexusOps (Phase 8 / BrainEventMapping, currently deferred).
findings.evidence: PRD.md:§8.2; MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:§9 ('Still open platform-side ... BrainEventMapping ... a live design surface on both sides'); NexusOps/docs/architecture/PROJECT_BRAIN_INTERFACE.md (no transition graph)
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm with the owner / NexusOps whether the frozen R-7 contract includes the WorkflowInstance transition graph or only the state set. If only the set is frozen, the Brain authors transitions for its own heuristic emission; if the graph is also frozen, mirror it exactly and add a contract-snapshot test (IR-1).

dimension: D3 — Unhandled FAILURE MODES / error paths
summary: The draft's §18 "Failure modes & recovery" is a one-paragraph restatement of 8 happy-ish recovery cases and explicitly delegates detail to RISKS.md + USER_FLOWS F10. Cross-checking against my assigned checklist, the planning corpus actually covers most named modes somewhere — but several are either (a) entirely absent from ALL artifacts, or (b) present only as narrative in USER_FLOWS and NEVER lifted into the architecture body as a contract/invariant with an observable signal. For a production-grade, no-timebox build, error paths are baseline, so a mode that lives only in a flow sentence is underspecified. CRITICAL absences (no behavior anywhere): disk-full / ENOSPC during ingest/optimize/blue-green, and model rate-limit/429/timeout (distinct from "API down"). IMPORTANT under-specs: sidecar crash/restart supervision + in-flight-request semantics (only "recycle" exists, which is a maintenance action not crash recovery); single-writer commit-conflict handling for the LEGITIMATE writer (watcher vs git-hook concurrency); federation partial-failure result-shape contract; CodeGraph schema-mismatch read-gating behavior; keychain-denied AT RUNTIME (not just setup); and the partial-ingest + redactor-quarantine paths that §18 omits despite F2/F7 naming them. These mostly need to be promoted into §18/§7 as named contracts before /tasks-gen can decompose them, and several change owner-confirmable behavior.

findings.finding: Disk-full / ENOSPC is an entirely unhandled failure mode — zero occurrences of disk-full/out-of-space/ENOSPC/quota in ANY planning artifact. D-25 W2 names 'versions accrue → TB bloat' as the MOTIVATION for cleanup_old_versions(), and the threat model T-11 covers 'unclean-quit data loss' via blue-green, but neither specifies what happens when a LanceDB write, optimize(), or blue-green generation copy FAILS because the disk is actually full. Blue-green re-embed (D-14/PH-5) writes a whole NEW generation before atomic swap — that doubles transient disk for a large project precisely when a portfolio of many repos is most likely to exhaust space, and there is no pre-flight space check, no graceful-abort-keeping-prior-generation contract for the ENOSPC case, and no user-surfaced remediation.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§18; DECISIONS.md:D-25 (W2 'TB bloat'); DECISIONS.md:D-14 (blue-green new generation); grep across docs/planning returns zero disk-full/ENOSPC hits
findings.proposed_fix: Add a named failure mode to §18 and the LanceDB maintenance contract (§7/PH-1): pre-flight free-space estimate before blue-green/index-build (size of new generation + headroom); on ENOSPC mid-write, abort the in-progress generation, retain the prior active generation (reuse the crash-mid-reindex invariant), surface a disk-full status with cleanup_old_versions() remediation; make 'low disk' a Project/worker state (degraded) rather than an unhandled crash.

findings.finding: Model rate-limit / 429 / timeout is conflated with 'model API down' and has no distinct handling. The only failure contract is 'model API down → retrieval-only degraded answer' (§18, F3, PH-9). Rate-limiting (429 with Retry-After), per-request timeouts, and mid-stream disconnects are transient/partial failures that warrant retry-with-backoff, partial-result handling, and a distinct user signal — NOT an immediate hard fallback to retrieval-only. This matters doubly because generation is cloud Claude by default (D-23) and embedding can be cloud Voyage (opt-in), so BOTH the index path (embed) and the answer path (generate) can hit 429s; the embed-side rate-limit during a large multi-repo ingest is completely unaddressed (could silently stall or fail an ingest batch).
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§18; USER_FLOWS.md:F3 ('model/API down → degrade to retrieval-only'); REQUIREMENTS.md:PH-9; DECISIONS.md:D-23 (cloud generation/embed); grep returns zero rate-limit/429/backoff/retry hits
findings.proposed_fix: Specify a provider-call resilience contract in §13/§18: bounded retry-with-exponential-backoff honoring Retry-After on 429; per-request timeout budgets distinct from 'down'; on embed-side rate-limit during ingest, pause+resume from the content-hash manifest (idempotent) rather than fail the batch; only after exhausting retries fall back to retrieval-only (generation) or mark the ingest batch incomplete (embed). Add a 'rate-limited/backing-off' observable status.

findings.finding: Sidecar crash/restart has no supervision or recovery contract. The desktop app is two processes (Tauri shell ↔ Python core sidecar, D-17) and the integrated mode is a 'daemon-owned lifecycle' sidecar (MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE §2). The ONLY mention of restart is D-25 W5 'periodically recycle the sidecar' — a planned maintenance action, not crash recovery. Missing: who supervises/respawns the sidecar on an unexpected crash (Tauri host? launchd? the NexusOps daemon?), what happens to an in-flight ask/ingest when the sidecar dies, whether the loopback per-launch token is rotated on respawn (D-5 — a respawn implies a new launch token the Tauri shell must re-handshake), and crash-loop backoff. 'Worker crash → idempotent resume' (F6/F10) covers the index WORKER but not the whole sidecar PROCESS hosting router+agent+MCP.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md:D-17 (two-process), D-25 W5 ('recycle the sidecar'), D-5 (per-launch token); MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:§2 (daemon-owned lifecycle); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§18 (no sidecar-process recovery); USER_FLOWS.md:F10 (worker crash only)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a sidecar lifecycle/supervision section: standalone = Tauri host supervises+respawns with crash-loop backoff and token re-handshake; integrated = NexusOps daemon owns it (cite ADR-005); define in-flight-request behavior (fail the open request with a typed error, never silent hang) and reconnect semantics for the desktop UI and for external MCP callers on stdio (process death = transport close).

findings.finding: LanceDB commit-conflict is only ever discussed as the JUSTIFICATION for a read-only federation router (D-25 W3: 'optimistic-commit conflicts — this is why the read-only router is correct'). But the LEGITIMATE single writer can still self-conflict: the watcher (F6) and the git-hooks backstop (post-commit/merge/checkout) can fire near-simultaneously on the same project, and a re-embed/blue-green can overlap an incremental sync. 'Single-writer-per-dataset' (Law 5/PH-1) is asserted as an invariant but the MECHANISM that serializes these concurrent triggers (a per-project write lock/queue) and the behavior on a lost optimistic commit (retry vs abort) is unspecified. Without it, two writers for one project is a real data-integrity path, not a hypothetical.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md:D-25 W3; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§4.2 Law 5 + §7 PH-1 ('single-writer'); USER_FLOWS.md:F6 (watcher AND git-hooks both trigger writes); grep: 'commit-conflict' only appears as federation rationale
findings.proposed_fix: Specify HOW single-writer-per-dataset is enforced for the legitimate writer: a per-project serialized write queue/lock that funnels watcher, git-hook, and re-embed triggers through one writer; define behavior on a lost optimistic commit (retry-from-manifest, bounded). Make this a tested invariant (it is currently asserted but un-mechanized).

findings.finding: Federation partial-failure is handled in narrative (F5: 'worker cold/evicted → cheap re-attach or skip-with-marker; schema mismatch → exclude + flag') but there is no result-shape CONTRACT for a degraded portfolio answer. Open questions: when 2 of 5 projects are excluded, does the answer carry which projects were skipped and why, with what confidence adjustment? Is a partial federation result distinguishable from a complete one by the caller (MCP/agent/drawer)? RRF rank-fusion (§9) over a SUBSET silently changes ranking — the provenance packet (DATA_MODEL) has drift_markers but no 'projects_excluded'/'coverage' field. For the trust north star (D-6), a silently-partial portfolio answer is a confident-but-incomplete failure analogous to PR-1.
findings.evidence: USER_FLOWS.md:F5 (skip-with-marker, narrative); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§9; DATA_MODEL.md:Provenance Packet (has drift_markers, no excluded-projects/coverage field)
findings.proposed_fix: Add an explicit federation-coverage contract to the Provenance Packet and §9: a 'projects_requested vs projects_answered + excluded[{project_id, reason}]' field surfaced on every federated answer; mark partial-coverage answers distinctly (the federation analogue of the grounding gate) so a caller never mistakes a 3/5 answer for a complete one.

findings.finding: CodeGraph schema-mismatch read-gating is named as a control but its BEHAVIOR is unspecified. RISKS TR-6 says 'read-only gating on schema_versions'; DATA_MODEL ② says 'we gate reads on schema_versions' and pins 'observed schema_versions v4'; the cross-repo resolution seam reads unresolved_refs + qualified_name. But there is no statement of what the gate DOES on a mismatch: does the whole project drop out of retrieval, or only the graph leg (degrade to vector-only for that repo), or does it block the federated query? §18's 'Schema/model mismatch → exclude + flag' appears to be about the LanceDB store-level stamp, not CodeGraph's external schema. The two are conflated. A CodeGraph upgrade that bumps v4→v5 mid-portfolio is a likely real event with undefined blast radius.
findings.evidence: RISKS.md:TR-6; DATA_MODEL.md:② ('schema_versions v4', 'gate reads on schema_versions'); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§18 ('Schema/model mismatch → exclude + flag' — ambiguous which store)
findings.proposed_fix: Disambiguate two distinct gates in §7/§9/§18: (a) LanceDB store stamp mismatch → exclude project + flag; (b) CodeGraph schema_versions mismatch → degrade THAT repo to vector-only (drop the graph leg, keep semantic retrieval) + flag 'structural tools unavailable for project X', not a full project exclusion. State the supported CodeGraph schema range explicitly so an upgrade is a detectable, handled event.

findings.finding: Keychain-denied is handled only at SETUP time ('keychain denied → prompt, don't store in plaintext', F1/setup). There is no runtime contract for keychain access being denied/revoked DURING normal operation — e.g., the OS revokes the access grant, the user clicks Deny on a runtime keychain prompt, or the keychain is locked. At runtime the SecretStore is dereferenced to call cloud providers (Voyage embed / Claude generate). If that read fails mid-ask or mid-ingest, behavior is undefined: does it hard-fail, fall back to local-only (degraded), or retry? Given hybrid posture defaults to cloud generation (D-23), a runtime keychain-denied silently breaking every cloud answer is a plausible, unhandled path.
findings.evidence: USER_FLOWS.md:F1 (keychain denied at setup only); DECISIONS.md:D-5/D-15 (keychain-only secrets); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§18 (no runtime keychain-denied path); THREAT_MODEL.md:T-3
findings.proposed_fix: Add a runtime SecretStore-unavailable failure mode: on keychain read denied/locked at query/ingest time, degrade to local-only (skip cloud generation/embed) with a clear 'cloud disabled: keychain access denied' status and a re-grant prompt, rather than failing the request; never fall back to any plaintext path (preserve T-3).

findings.finding: Partial-ingest and redactor-quarantine paths are specified in USER_FLOWS (F2: 'partial ingest → temp generation, no half-swapped index; corrupt file → skip + log, don't abort'; F7/T-1: 'high-confidence-unsafe span → quarantine, don't embed') but are NOT lifted into the architecture body §18, which omits both. For a production-grade build these are first-class error paths the implementer must build to. Additionally, the quarantine path lacks a downstream contract: a quarantined span means that chunk is NOT in the index — is the gap surfaced (a 'redaction hole'/coverage signal), or does the answer silently lack content it should have cited? A silently-missing quarantined chunk could produce an under-grounded answer with no signal — a subtle trust failure.
findings.evidence: USER_FLOWS.md:F2 (partial ingest / corrupt-file skip), F7 + THREAT_MODEL.md:T-1 (quarantine); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§18 (neither appears); DATA_MODEL.md (no quarantine/coverage field)
findings.proposed_fix: Promote both into §18 as named contracts: partial-ingest → temp generation never atomically swapped (reuse blue-green invariant), corrupt/unparseable file → skip+log+count, surface a per-project ingest-completeness signal. For quarantine: record a per-project quarantined-span count and surface it so retrieval coverage gaps are visible (an answer over a partially-quarantined corpus should not present as fully grounded).

findings.finding: §18 'Failure modes & recovery' is tagged [LOCKED] yet is a lossy one-paragraph compression that omits at least 6 modes its own cited sources (RISKS, F10, THREAT_MODEL) contain (disk-full, rate-limit, sidecar-process crash, legitimate-writer commit-conflict, federation-coverage, runtime-keychain-denied). A [LOCKED] section that under-covers its delegated sources risks /tasks-gen decomposing only the 8 listed paths and dropping the rest, since tasks-gen anchors to the architecture body, not the supporting artifacts.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§18 (8 cases, '[LOCKED]', 'Detail: RISKS.md, USER_FLOWS F10'); cross-ref RISKS.md/USER_FLOWS.md/THREAT_MODEL.md contain more
findings.proposed_fix: Expand §18 into a complete failure-mode table (mode · trigger · detection signal · recovery behavior · user-surfaced status · test) covering all D3-1..D3-8 modes, so the binding ARCHITECTURE.md carries every error path tasks-gen must decompose rather than delegating to artifacts tasks-gen won't anchor to.

findings.finding: For the blue-green disk-doubling concern (D3-1): is the intended blue-green strategy a full new-generation COPY (which doubles disk for the project, the assumption behind D3-1), or does it exploit LanceDB native versioning so the new generation shares unchanged data files and only the changed/re-embedded deltas are rewritten (much smaller transient footprint)? D-14 says 'never in place... new generation + atomic swap' and DATA_MODEL says 'no in-place re-embed', but whether 'new generation' means a physical copy or a Lance version-on-top is undetermined and materially changes the disk-full risk and the pre-flight space-check design.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md:D-14 ('new generation + atomic swap'); DATA_MODEL.md:invariant 2 ('no in-place re-embed... new versioned generation'); DECISIONS.md:D-11 (LanceDB 'native versioning/time-travel')
findings.proposed_fix: Owner/architect to confirm whether blue-green = physical copy vs Lance native version layering; document the transient disk multiplier explicitly so D3-1's pre-flight space-check and the cleanup_old_versions() cadence can be sized correctly.

dimension: D4 — Underspecified INTERFACES / SCHEMAS / data contracts
summary: The draft NAMES every load-bearing contract but DEFINES almost none to a freeze-able level. The two highest-stakes interfaces — the MCP tool contract (the trust boundary AND the eventual NexusOps seam) and the port method signatures (HostPort/EmbeddingProvider/Reranker/ContextStrategy/ModelProvider/ObservabilitySink/SecretStore/EventSource) — appear only as bare names with zero parameter/return/error shapes anywhere across all 18 planning artifacts. The manifest schema is explicitly OPEN (OQ-6) yet is a day-one sibling-to-scaffolding + federation-registry-read artifact that must freeze before the first ingest slice; worse, its FILENAME is inconsistent across docs (`.project-brain.json` vs `.project-brain/manifest.json`). policy.yaml is referenced as the per-project provider/privacy contract and as a manifest pointer but is NEVER schematized. The provenance packet fields differ between DATA_MODEL and PRD §7.10. The LanceDB chunk schema is defined but missing fields the architecture's own flows require (blue-green generation marker, tombstone state, episode-card linkage). On the NexusOps seam, the brain must emit ~9 of a frozen 10-value ProjectBrain status vocabulary and serialize evidence to a lossy closed 11-value EvidenceType, but neither the status enum mapping nor the lossy EvidenceType mapping is captured as a binding cross-doc invariant. These are the contracts that MUST become Appendix-A cross-doc invariants before /tasks-gen, or parallel tracks will build against incompatible shapes.

findings.finding: The MCP tool contract — the single most load-bearing interface (it is BOTH the standalone trust boundary AND the NexusOps integration seam) — is named only as `search`/`get_file`/`graph`/`list_projects`/`status` with NO parameter schemas, return shapes, error semantics, pagination/streaming contract, or scope-filter parameter defined anywhere across all 18 planning artifacts. The seam doc states the drawer 'implies the Brain query API should accept a retrieval-scope parameter' but that parameter is never specified. Flow 4 says 'structured results carry stable IDs + file:line; large search/graph stream' but no schema backs it. This cannot be built or frozen, and two surfaces (embedded agent + MCP) plus the future NexusOpsHost all depend on it.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§11; REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-12; USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 4; MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:§6 (scope chips constrain retrieval)
findings.proposed_fix: Add an Appendix-A cross-doc invariant defining each MCP tool's full signature: params (incl. the retrieval-scope filter enum, project-id scoping, top-k), result shape (evidence chip + file:line + stable IDs + provenance packet), the streaming contract for large search/graph, and error/deny semantics (e.g. policy-denied project → marker not error, per Flow 4). Pin FastMCP 3.x tool-registration shape (D-24).

findings.finding: The eight ports (HostPort, EventSource, EmbeddingProvider, Reranker, ContextStrategy, ModelProvider, ObservabilitySink, SecretStore) are the entire ports-and-adapters spine (D-21) and the standalone↔embedded swap point, but NOT ONE has a defined method signature. HostPort is described only by behavior ('authorize + perform side-effects', 'bounded allowlist vs propose-only') — there is no `authorize(action) -> Decision`, no `perform(mutation) -> Result`, no mapping of the StandaloneHost allowlist members (own store / owned-doc refresh / host-config) to typed methods, and no shape for how NexusOpsHost turns a mutation into a `submit_action_plan` call. The whole 'embedded = additive adapter, zero core refactor' claim (D-21) is unverifiable without the port signature being frozen day one.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§4.3/§6/§12; DECISIONS.md:D-21; DOMAIN_MODEL.md:Host glossary entry; CLAUDE_CODE_HANDOFF.md:item 3 (explicitly flags HostPort/port contracts as a finalize target)
findings.proposed_fix: Define each port as a binding interface in an Appendix-A invariant: HostPort.authorize/perform/capabilities; EmbeddingProvider.embed/dimension/model_version; Reranker.rerank; ContextStrategy.augment; ModelProvider.generate (with Citations API contract); ObservabilitySink.emit; SecretStore.get_ref/resolve; EventSource.subscribe/poll (git/watcher vs outbox). For HostPort, enumerate the StandaloneHost allowlist as typed mutation variants and show the NexusOpsHost serialization to ActionPlan.

findings.finding: The `.project-brain` manifest has a FILENAME inconsistency that is load-bearing because the file is a frozen artifact (sibling to read-only `.scaffolding/manifest.json`, read by the federation registry, never-mutate boundary). ONBOARDING_AND_DOC_LIFECYCLE R-ADD says write `.project-brain.json`; DATA_MODEL/DOMAIN_MODEL/REQUIREMENTS/USER_FLOWS/PRD §8.3 all say `.project-brain/manifest.json` (a directory). These are different on-disk contracts (single file vs directory). Unresolved, ingest, sync, uninstall, and the read-only/owned boundary tests will disagree.
findings.evidence: ONBOARDING_AND_DOC_LIFECYCLE.md:R-ADD step 2 (`.project-brain.json`); DATA_MODEL.md:③; PRD.md:§8.3; REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-6; USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 2 step 6
findings.proposed_fix: Pick one (recommend `.project-brain/manifest.json` directory form, consistent with the majority and with allowing sibling files like `policy.yaml`) and correct ONBOARDING R-ADD. Record as a cross-doc invariant naming the exact path.

findings.finding: The per-project `policy.yaml` schema is undefined. It is referenced as the provider+privacy contract chosen at `add` (D-23), the MCP-boundary policy filter honored regardless of caller (D-5/T-6/FR-13), the manifest pointer `policy_path` (DATA_MODEL), and the registry field `policy` — i.e. it gates retrieval results, redaction, cloud opt-out, and federation visibility. Yet no document specifies its fields (embedding/rerank/context/model provider selections, cloud training-opt-out flags, session-ingestion consent, `.brainignore` linkage, per-project privacy posture, project-visibility for federation). A security-load-bearing config with no schema cannot be validated, tested, or frozen.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md:D-23 (per-project policy.yaml at add); REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-13; DATA_MODEL.md:③ (policy_path, registry `policy`); DOMAIN_MODEL.md:Policy glossary; THREAT_MODEL.md:T-6
findings.proposed_fix: Define policy.yaml as an Appendix-A schema: providers{embedding,reranker,context,model + version pins}, privacy{cloud_training_optout: enforced, zdr_required, local_only_generation}, ingestion{sessions_consented: bool}, visibility{federation_exposed: bool}, ignore{brainignore_path}. State which fields the MCP boundary enforces and which the redactor consumes.

findings.finding: The brain must emit a ProjectBrain status report, but the frozen NexusOps vocabulary is the closed 10-value set `not_configured, indexing, ready, partial_index, stale, graph_degraded, transcript_ingestion_off, transcript_ingestion_active, reindex_required, error` (platform derives `stale`, so brain emits the other 9). The brain docs only say '~10-token health vocabulary' / 'emit the other ~9' and NEVER enumerate them, NOR map them to the brain's own internal Project state machine (`added→indexing→ready⇄syncing→drift_detected→reindexing→archived/removed`) + Worker (`cold→warm→idle-evicted`). The two state vocabularies do not align 1:1 (e.g. brain `syncing`/`drift_detected`/`reindexing`/`archived/removed` have no platform target; platform `partial_index`/`graph_degraded`/`transcript_ingestion_*`/`reindex_required` have no brain source). No mapping = wrong status emitted at the seam.
findings.evidence: MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:§5 reverse direction; NexusOps/ARCHITECTURE.md:§5.1 line 198 (frozen 10-value ProjectBrain machine); DATA_MODEL.md:State machines (brain Project + Worker machines)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a binding status-mapping table: brain internal state → emitted ProjectBrain status (one of the 9 emittable). Resolve which brain states collapse (syncing→indexing? drift_detected→reindex_required?) and how `partial_index`/`graph_degraded`/`transcript_ingestion_*` are derived. Make it an Appendix-A invariant since it is a frozen cross-product contract.

findings.finding: The Evidence Chip → EvidenceRef serialization is lossy and the brain's richer chip vocabulary (13 types in PRD §7.2) does NOT map onto the closed 11-value EvidenceType cleanly — PRD §7.2 documents that 'Agent team run' and 'Decision record' are ResourceRef (a ResourceType) NOT EvidenceTypes, and 'Test result' has NO platform-backed evidence type. But DOMAIN_MODEL and DATA_MODEL describe Evidence Chip simply as 'serializes to NexusOps EvidenceRef (11-value EvidenceType) at the seam' WITHOUT carrying the lossy/fallback mapping into the binding data contract. Building the serializer from the data-model docs alone would silently drop or mis-type 3 chip kinds.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:Evidence Chip / Reference; DOMAIN_MODEL.md:Evidence glossary; PRD.md:§7.2 (lossy mapping notes); MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:§5 (EvidenceRef{type,label,resource_ref?,confidence?})
findings.proposed_fix: Promote the full PRD §7.2 chip→EvidenceType/ResourceRef mapping table into a binding cross-doc invariant referenced by DATA_MODEL, including the Test-result/Agent-team-run/Decision-record fallbacks and the EvidenceRef wire shape `{type,label,resource_ref?,confidence?}`.

findings.finding: The Provenance Packet field set is inconsistent between DATA_MODEL and PRD §7.10/PB-10. DATA_MODEL lists `{project_ids, source_ids, file:line[], commit_shas, session_ids, ingested_from_sha, index_freshness, confidence_markers, drift_markers, low_confidence_links}` — it OMITS `timestamps` and uses `low_confidence_links`; PRD §7.10 lists `Timestamps` and `Low-confidence association markers` and omits nothing equivalently. The provenance packet is the audit-of-trust attached to every answer (north star D-6) and the basis for evidence chips — its field set must be a single frozen shape.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:Provenance Packet (Core domain objects); PRD.md:§7.10 / PB-10 (lines 769-781)
findings.proposed_fix: Reconcile to one canonical Provenance Packet schema (decide timestamps in/out; unify low_confidence_links vs low-confidence-association-markers naming) and bind it once as an Appendix-A invariant.

findings.finding: The LanceDB chunk schema (the build-critical table) is defined but missing fields the architecture's own flows require. (a) Blue-green re-embed / atomic-swap (D-14, Flow 9, Index-generation state machine) needs a generation/version marker on or alongside chunks, but the chunk record carries none and the store-level stamp has no generation id. (b) Tombstone+replace keyed on source_path (Flow 6, R-DOCLIFECYCLE) implies a tombstone/active state, absent from the schema. (c) The dual-register `register: plain|deep` field exists but how plain vs deep variants coexist as separate chunks/rows vs one row is unspecified. (d) Episode-card linkage to chunks and the `linked_tasks` field (PRD §7.4 vs DATA_MODEL omits linked_tasks) is unmodeled in the store.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:① chunk record + Index-generation state machine; USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 6/Flow 9; DECISIONS.md:D-14; PRD.md:§7.4 (linked_tasks present; DATA_MODEL Episode Card omits it)
findings.proposed_fix: Extend the chunk/store schema with: an explicit generation id (ties to the version stamp + git-SHA tag), an active/tombstoned marker (or document that tombstone = LanceDB delete + version), the plain/deep dual-register storage decision, and reconcile Episode Card fields (add linked_tasks) plus its store representation. Freeze as the LanceDB chunk-schema invariant.

findings.finding: The manifest schema is explicitly OPEN (OQ-6, 'freeze at first ingest slice') yet it is a day-one frozen artifact: it is the never-mutate sibling to `.scaffolding/manifest.json`, its `artifacts[]` carry the owned/foreign/supplemental classification that gates the doc-lifecycle write boundary (T-9), and the global registry derived from it is what the federation router fans out on (D-2). Several fields are named (schemaVersion, project_id, ingestedFromSha, embedding_model, dimension, chunker_version, doc_format_spec_range, artifacts[], staleness_pointer, policy_path, lance_version_tag) but types, required-vs-optional, and the registry-vs-manifest field split are not pinned, and `staleness_pointer`/`doc_format_spec_range` are undefined terms.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:③; OPEN_QUESTIONS.md:OQ-6; THREAT_MODEL.md:T-9; DECISIONS.md:D-2
findings.proposed_fix: Close OQ-6 in finalize rather than deferring to first slice (it blocks parallel ingest/registry/federation tracks): pin field types, required/optional, define staleness_pointer and doc_format_spec_range, and specify exactly which fields live in the per-repo manifest vs the global registry row. Bind as a cross-doc invariant.

findings.finding: The Action Plan internal→wire mapping (PRD §7.10/§10.426) is the propose-only seam contract but the FROZEN ActionPlan shape `{plan_id(aplan_<ULID>), title, steps:ActionPlanStep[], dependencies:ActionDependency[], overall_risk(0–4), approval_mode}` and ActionPlanStep/ActionDependency field shapes are described in PRD prose but not carried into the brain's binding data model (DATA_MODEL lists 'Implementation Plan / Plan Task / Workflow Pack / Workflow Instance — per PRD §7.5–7.8' but Action Plan is absent from DATA_MODEL's schema section entirely). The brain's proposed `action_type` set must also be a verified subset of the frozen NexusOps catalog (the as-built 28; brain doc cites the MVP ~21), which is asserted but not enumerated/checked anywhere.
findings.evidence: PRD.md:§7.10 NexusOps-alignment (wire shape) + §9.3; DATA_MODEL.md:Core domain objects (Action Plan only in DOMAIN_MODEL prose, no schema); NexusOps/ARCHITECTURE.md:§6.3 (28 as-built action types)
findings.proposed_fix: Bind the ActionPlan/ActionPlanStep/ActionDependency wire shape (generate Pydantic from the published nexusops-contract JSON-Schema per PRD OQ-6-resolved) and add an explicit enumerated subset check: which brain.* + catalog action_types the planner may emit, verified against the frozen catalog. Note this is [P2] but the seam contract should be pinned now since C-15 requires conforming to frozen primitives.

findings.finding: The BrainEventMapping (MCP notification → platform event_type) and the Brain outbox payload shape are frozen forward targets the brain must conform to, but the brain docs only name the notifications (`resources/updated`, `…/list_changed`, Tasks status, ProjectBrain status report) and the target events (`BrainIndexStarted/Completed/Failed`, `BrainSourceIngested`) WITHOUT specifying which brain-side signal raises which notification, the read-back call the adapter makes, or the outbox-consumption envelope the brain ingests (order-by-seq, dedup on event_id/idempotency_key, deny-unknown-fields, tolerate-unknown-event_type). PB-9 lists these behaviors in prose but no consumption-contract schema is pinned.
findings.evidence: MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:§4 (outbox) + §5 (notifications); PRD.md:PB-9; NexusOps/ARCHITECTURE.md:§13.1 (BrainEventMapping, outbox payload = envelope minus restricted/secret)
findings.proposed_fix: Pin the brain-side notification trigger map (index lifecycle event → which MCP notification) and the outbox-consumer contract (EventEnvelope subset fields brain reads, seq-order, dedup key, unknown-tolerance) as an Appendix-A invariant flagged [P2 co-design]. Cite the frozen EventEnvelope (16 required fields, CONTRACT 0.34.0).

findings.finding: The CodeGraph read interface the brain depends on is described as a SQLite schema (nodes/edges/files/unresolved_refs/nodes_fts at observed schema_versions v4) and 'read-only WAL / one-shot CLI / MCP', but the brain's actual structural-tool calls (callers/callees/impact/trace, used always-on in retrieval per D-13/Flow 3/5) have no defined call contract — are they SQL queries against the observed schema, CLI invocations, or MCP tool calls, and what is the result shape the fusion step consumes? The federation cross-repo seam (unresolved_refs.reference_name + namespaced qualified_name) is a spike (O-FED), acceptable, but the per-repo structural-tool contract is build-critical and unspecified.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:② CodeGraph; DECISIONS.md:D-13 (graph tools always-on); USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 3 step 2 / Flow 5 step 2; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§8
findings.proposed_fix: Specify the CodeGraph access adapter: the four structural operations (callers/callees/impact/trace) as typed calls, their backing mechanism (SQL on the v4 schema vs CLI vs MCP), and the result shape the query-time fusion consumes. Gate reads on schema_versions as already stated.

findings.finding: The store-level version stamp is specified in two non-identical forms: DATA_MODEL says `{embedding_model, dimension, schema_version, index_built_at, source_root_hash, ingested_from_sha}` while USER_FLOWS Flow 2 step 5 stamps `{embedding_model, dim, schema_version, index_built_at, source_root_hash}` (drops ingested_from_sha) and FR-5 stamps `{model, dim, schema, sha}`. The reproducibility invariant (NFR-2/DATA_MODEL §reproducibility) hinges on this exact set. Is ingested_from_sha part of the store stamp, the chunk record (it is also a per-chunk field), or both?
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:Store-level version stamp + chunk record; USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 2 step 5; REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-5
findings.proposed_fix: Owner/finalizer to confirm the canonical store-stamp field set and whether ingested_from_sha is store-level, chunk-level, or both, then unify across DATA_MODEL/USER_FLOWS/REQUIREMENTS.

findings.finding: `.brainignore` is referenced as a first-class ingestion-scope control (PB-2, FR-2, Flow 2, R-DISCOVERY, policy glossary) but its format/precedence relative to .gitignore is never specified (gitignore-syntax? does it add to or override .gitignore? does it live at repo root or in .project-brain/?). Minor but it is a user-facing contract that ingest tests will need pinned.
findings.evidence: REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-2; USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 2 step 2; ONBOARDING_AND_DOC_LIFECYCLE.md:R-DISCOVERY; PRD.md:PB-2
findings.proposed_fix: Specify .brainignore: gitignore-glob syntax, additive-to-.gitignore precedence, location, and the always-skip set (vendored/build/secrets). One sentence in the manifest/policy invariant suffices.

dimension: D5 — Source-of-truth clarity (authoritative owner per piece of state; dual-write / ambiguous ownership)
summary: The draft is strong on the two highest-stakes ownership questions: the index-is-a-cache law (LanceDB derived from source), CodeGraph read-only ownership, single-writer-per-index, and episode-card ownership (Brain-owned, no platform event) are all unambiguous and consistent across DATA_MODEL/DECISIONS/DOMAIN_MODEL/THREAT_MODEL and the NexusOps ground truth. However, the FRESHNESS/VERSION metadata is triple-written with no declared authoritative owner or derivation direction: the per-index git SHA exists as chunk-level `ingested_from_sha`/`last_resolved_sha`, store-level stamp `ingested_from_sha`, manifest `ingestedFromSha`/`staleness_pointer`/`lance_version_tag`, AND registry `last_indexed_sha` — four copies, no reconciliation rule. The same dual-write affects schema_version/model_version (store stamp vs manifest vs registry), and the federation router gates on the REGISTRY copy while the actual truth lives in the LanceDB store stamp — a stale-gate hazard. The Anchor's storage owner is ambiguous (embedded in the chunk row vs a separate store). There is also a manifest-filename inconsistency (`.project-brain/manifest.json` vs `.project-brain.json`). For a production-grade, no-timebox build where freshness ("never stale silently") is the north star's load-bearing support, these ownership ambiguities are correctness/operability gaps, not deferrable polish.

findings.finding: The per-index git SHA / freshness pointer is written in FOUR places with no declared authoritative owner or derivation direction. It appears as chunk-level `ingested_from_sha` + `last_resolved_sha` (LanceDB rows), the store-level version stamp `{...ingested_from_sha}` (one per dataset), the manifest `ingestedFromSha` + `staleness_pointer` + `lance_version_tag`, AND the registry row `last_indexed_sha`. Nothing states which is canonical, which is derived, or how they are kept consistent after a sync/crash. Architectural Law 1 says the index is reproducible from source + model-stamp, but the *freshness* facts about that index live in 3-4 stores that can silently diverge (e.g., manifest written but registry update crashes, or LanceDB tagged but manifest not updated). Because freshness ('never stale silently', D-6/D-14) is the load-bearing support beneath the trust north star, a divergence here means the system can report a stale SHA as current — exactly the cardinal failure.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:① chunk fields (last_resolved_sha, ingested_from_sha) + store-level stamp + ③ manifest (ingestedFromSha, staleness_pointer, lance_version_tag) + Global registry (last_indexed_sha); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§7; DECISIONS.md:D-14
findings.proposed_fix: Declare one authoritative owner for the index's current SHA/freshness and make the others explicitly derived projections. Recommended: the LanceDB version tag (git-SHA, GC-exempt per D-25) is the single source of truth for 'what SHA this generation is at'; the store-level stamp is the canonical schema/model/dim record; the manifest and registry rows are DERIVED CACHES rebuilt from the LanceDB stamp/tags on every successful index commit (and reconcilable by re-reading the dataset on startup). State the write-order + crash-reconciliation rule (e.g., LanceDB commit is the commit point; manifest+registry are best-effort projections re-derived if they disagree with the dataset).

findings.finding: The federation router gates each store on `schema_version`/`model_version` read from the REGISTRY, but the authoritative schema/model/dim values live in the LanceDB store-level stamp. The registry is a separate hand-maintained copy (`project_id → {schema_version, model_version, ...}`). If a project is re-embedded (blue-green, new model/dim) but the registry row is not updated in lockstep, the router will gate (admit or refuse) on stale metadata — admitting a dim-mismatched store into a union query or wrongly excluding a healthy one. No invariant binds the registry copy to the store stamp.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:Global registry (`schema_version, model_version`) + 'what the federation router reads to fan out'; DATA_MODEL.md:Federation read model ('Gate every store on schema_version/model_version'); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§9; store-level stamp in DATA_MODEL.md:①
findings.proposed_fix: Make the router gate on the store's OWN stamp (read from the opened LanceDB dataset at fan-out time), not on the registry copy — the registry should only be a routing INDEX (which datasets exist + paths), with schema/model treated as a hint that is re-verified against the dataset before admitting it. Alternatively, declare the registry strictly derived from store stamps and rebuilt on every index commit, with a startup reconciliation pass. State this as an invariant.

findings.finding: The per-project manifest and the global registry are a classic dual-write: the same per-project facts (SHA, schema_version, model_version, policy, db paths) are stored in BOTH `.project-brain/manifest.json` (per repo) and `~/.project-brain/registry` (global), with no statement of which is authoritative or how they reconcile. Manifest has `{schemaVersion, ingestedFromSha, embedding_model, dimension, policy_path, lance_version_tag}`; registry has `{schema_version, model_version, last_indexed_sha, policy}`. A repo could be moved/restored/hand-edited (manifest changes) without the registry knowing, or the registry rebuilt without re-reading manifests. No rebuild/repair path is specified (e.g., 'registry is reconstructable by scanning manifests' or vice versa).
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:③ Catalog (manifest schema + Global registry schema); DOMAIN_MODEL.md:Manifest + Registry glossary entries; PRODUCT_BRIEF.md:64 (registry row vs manifest)
findings.proposed_fix: Declare the per-project manifest the source of truth for per-project facts (it lives WITH the repo and survives moves), and the global registry a DERIVED INDEX rebuilt by scanning manifests (`add` writes manifest then upserts registry; a `repair`/startup pass reconciles registry from manifests). Or the inverse — but pick one and state the rebuild direction + the reconcile trigger. Add it to the failure-modes section (§18) as a recovery path.

findings.finding: Anchor storage ownership is ambiguous. The chunk row carries an inline `anchor` field plus `last_resolved_sha` (DATA_MODEL ①), while a separate first-class `Anchor` domain object exists with its own id and richer fields `{anchor_id, source_file, source_span, target_path, target_line_*, target_symbol, state, last_resolved_sha, confidence}`. The draft never says whether the Anchor is (a) materialized as part of the LanceDB chunk row, (b) a separate table/store, or (c) computed on the fly at revalidation. This matters because the Anchor's `state` (live|stale|moved|unknown) is continuously revalidated and is THE trust primitive — its authoritative store determines whether revalidation is a write-back to LanceDB (touching the single-writer + optimize() contract on every sync) or a separate index. Two places hold `last_resolved_sha` (chunk and Anchor) with no stated owner.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:① chunk `anchor` + `last_resolved_sha` fields; DATA_MODEL.md:Core domain objects Anchor `{...last_resolved_sha...}`; DOMAIN_MODEL.md:Index 1──* Chunk 1──1 Anchor; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§5/§10
findings.proposed_fix: State where the Anchor lives and who owns its `state`. Recommended: the Anchor IS a projection of the chunk row + a live revalidation against source@SHA — `state`/`last_resolved_sha` are recomputed at sync and answer time and written back to the chunk row (single-writer), OR held in a lightweight side-index that is itself reproducible from chunks+source. Make the write-path explicit so revalidation's interaction with the LanceDB maintenance contract (optimize() / single-writer) is designed, not implied.

findings.finding: The manifest filename is inconsistent across the binding artifacts vs the onboarding requirement. The planning set (DATA_MODEL, DECISIONS D-12, DOMAIN_MODEL, REQUIREMENTS FR-6, PRODUCT_BRIEF) uniformly says `.project-brain/manifest.json` (a directory sibling to `.scaffolding/`). The onboarding requirement R-ADD step 2 says write `.project-brain.json` (a single dotfile). These are different on-disk artifacts. Whichever is the per-project source-of-truth catalog, the name must be one thing — a build that writes `.project-brain.json` while sync/discovery looks for `.project-brain/manifest.json` would silently never find its own manifest.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:③ `.project-brain/manifest.json`; DECISIONS.md:D-12; REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-6; ONBOARDING_AND_DOC_LIFECYCLE.md:R-ADD step 2 (`.project-brain.json`)
findings.proposed_fix: Reconcile to `.project-brain/manifest.json` (the planning-set consensus; a directory also gives room for the policy.yaml, lock files, and future per-project state). Update R-ADD's `.project-brain.json` reference, or explicitly note the onboarding doc is superseded (as D-20 already does for its `npm i -g` line).

findings.finding: The drift radar / sync convergence has an implicit dual-truth for 'what is current' that is not pinned: freshness is driven by the watcher (Watchman/fswatch) for latency and git-hooks (post-commit/merge/checkout) for convergence, and drift is computed by diffing indexed content-hashes against current source. But the AUTHORITATIVE 'current source SHA' is git HEAD, while the index's recorded SHA lives in the (multi-copy) stamp/manifest/registry per D5-1. The draft says 'every answer is grounded at a known SHA' but does not say WHICH recorded SHA is authoritative for the freshness banner when the watcher and the hook backstop disagree (e.g., watcher saw an uncommitted save; hook fires on a later commit). Without a declared owner for 'the SHA the index claims to be at', the freshness banner and grounding gate can present inconsistent freshness.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§10 (watcher=freshness, hooks=convergence); DECISIONS.md:D-14(4); DATA_MODEL.md:reproducibility invariant 5 ('grounded at a known SHA'); NFR-1 (REQUIREMENTS.md)
findings.proposed_fix: Define the index's recorded SHA as a single authoritative field (per D5-1, the LanceDB version tag) and specify that the freshness banner/grounding gate read freshness as `delta(git HEAD, index.recorded_sha)` — with uncommitted-working-tree changes surfaced as a distinct 'dirty since last index' state separate from the committed-SHA delta. Make watcher-driven partial reindex update the recorded SHA atomically with the LanceDB commit so the two signals can't disagree.

findings.finding: The CodeGraph `schema_versions` value that project-brain gates reads on (DATA_MODEL ②, 'we gate reads on schema_versions') is a piece of external state owned by CodeGraph, but the registry ALSO stores a `codegraph_db_path` and the router 'queries N CodeGraph DBs (read-only)'. There is no recorded copy of the CodeGraph schema version in the registry, so the gate is computed live each fan-out (good), but the draft does not say whether a CodeGraph version mismatch is cached/remembered or re-probed every query — an operability detail, not an ownership conflict. Worth pinning so the router doesn't re-probe N SQLite headers on every federated query.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:② ('gate reads on schema_versions') + Global registry (`codegraph_db_path`, no codegraph schema field); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§9
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm CodeGraph schema-version is re-probed live at fan-out (authoritative owner = CodeGraph, never cached as Brain truth), and optionally record a last-seen value + last-checked timestamp in the registry purely as a diagnostic (clearly marked non-authoritative). State the re-probe cadence to bound per-query overhead.

findings.finding: The provider version stamp's authoritative owner across the per-project `policy.yaml`, the LanceDB store-level stamp, the manifest (`embedding_model, dimension, chunker_version`), and the registry (`model_version`) is not declared. `policy.yaml` is described as the place a provider is CHOSEN at `add` (D-23), but the store stamp records what the index was ACTUALLY built with. On a provider switch, policy.yaml changes first (intent) and the store stamp changes only after blue-green re-embed completes (fact). The draft never states that policy.yaml = desired config and the store stamp = realized config, nor what happens to in-flight queries when they disagree (policy says voyage, store still qwen).
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md:D-23 (policy.yaml chosen at add; switching = blue-green); DATA_MODEL.md:① store stamp `{embedding_model, dimension}` + ③ manifest `{embedding_model, dimension, chunker_version}` + registry `{model_version}`; REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-5/PH-5
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm the intended split — recommended: `policy.yaml` = DESIRED provider config (owner of intent), the LanceDB store stamp = REALIZED config (owner of fact, what queries trust), with the manifest/registry copies derived from the store stamp (per D5-1/D5-3). Specify the transitional behavior during blue-green re-embed (old generation + old stamp serves until atomic swap; policy/stamp mismatch is the expected in-flight state). Owner should confirm whether this matches intent.

summary: I independently verified the external-dependency claims against current (2026-06-16) sources. The draft's research is mostly sound on model SOTA/licenses, but D6 surfaces several load-bearing gaps. The single most serious: CodeGraph — a `[locked]` external dependency the federation differentiator (D-2) is built on — shipped v1.0.0/v1.0.1 days ago (June 12-13, 2026) with documented breaking changes (config-file removal, MCP tools cut 8→4) AND a "workspace multi-repo indexing" feature that directly undercuts the planning premise that CodeGraph has NO native cross-repo support; the entire dossier and pins target the stale `0.9.7`. Second: the Voyage training opt-out that D-23/SR-2/C-12 require the cloud adapter to "enforce + document" is an account-level dashboard toggle (admin role + payment method on file), NOT a per-request API flag — it cannot be programmatically enforced as the threat model assumes, data sent before opt-out stays trainable forever, and opt-in is irreversible without contacting support. Third: the "cloud generation over ZDR" privacy posture (D-23/C-12) assumes ZDR is available/default, but Anthropic ZDR requires a separately-negotiated enterprise agreement (not toggleable, not on standard Enterprise) — the single-user local-first target almost certainly lacks it — and Fable-5/Mythos-class models (which the bundled claude-api guidance defaults toward) are not available under ZDR at all (30-day retention). Cost is entirely unquantified: no per-query or per-ingest dollar figure appears anywhere for the Claude agent loop (Opus 4.8 $5/$25 per 1M, agentic RAG 3-10x tokens, whole-file hydration, ≤200K long-context routing) or for the Voyage cloud path, despite production-grade posture. Minor: qwen3 Apache-2.0 carries an unresolved MS MARCO non-commercial fine-tuning-data ambiguity; FastMCP 3.0's breaking-change list (D-24) is incomplete.

findings.finding: CodeGraph — the `[locked]` external structural-index dependency that the federation differentiator is built on — shipped v1.0.0 (2026-06-12) and v1.0.1 (2026-06-13), DAYS before this draft, with documented breaking changes (config file `.codegraph/config.json` removed in 0.9.2; MCP default tool list cut from 8 to 4 in 1.0) AND a NEW 'workspace multi-repo indexing' capability. Every planning artifact instead targets `@colbymchenry/codegraph@0.9.7` and asserts as `[VERIFIED]` that CodeGraph has NO native multi-repo / cross-repo resolution (the premise the whole D-2 federation router + O-FED spike + 'fan out, not multiplex' design rests on). If 1.0 ships workspace multi-repo indexing, the load-bearing federation premise is potentially obsolete and the probe (against 0.9.7) is stale by a major version.
findings.evidence: github.com/colbymchenry/codegraph/releases (v1.0.1 2026-06-13; 0.9.2 config removal; 1.0 'workspace multi-repo indexing', MCP tools 8→4) vs RESEARCH_DOSSIER.md §2 'CodeGraph local probe' ([VERIFIED] @0.9.7, 'Multi-workspace — NO multiplexing; strictly one-index-per-repo'), CONSTRAINTS.md C-5, ASSUMPTIONS.md A-5, DECISIONS.md D-12, RESEARCH.md R4
findings.proposed_fix: Before finalizing, re-probe `@colbymchenry/codegraph@1.0.1`: (1) confirm whether 'workspace multi-repo indexing' subsumes or competes with the brain's federation router (re-scope or de-risk D-2/O-FED accordingly); (2) re-verify the SQLite schema (`schema_versions` version, the `nodes`/`edges`/`unresolved_refs` tables the read-only federation reads) survived the 1.0 bump — D-12 reads this DB directly, so a schema change is a silent data-plane break; (3) re-enumerate the now-4-tool MCP surface vs the callers/callees/impact/trace tools §8/§11 assume; (4) bump the pin in C-5/A-5/D-12 to a confirmed 1.x version and record the verified schema_version. Owner must confirm whether to keep building the federation router or lean on native multi-repo.

findings.finding: The Voyage training opt-out that D-23 / SR-2 / C-12 require the cloud adapter to 'enforce + document' and that THREAT_MODEL/RISKS gate the adapter on ('Adapter refuses cloud without opt-out confirmed') is an ACCOUNT-LEVEL dashboard toggle requiring an org-Admin role plus a payment method on file — it is NOT a per-request API flag/header and cannot be set, verified, or enforced programmatically by the sidecar. Worse: data sent BEFORE opting out 'may continue to be subject to' training rights (permanent), opt-in is irreversible without contacting support, and opting out may void free credits.
