The architecture's binding invariant 'cloud training opt-out enforced' (§15) is therefore not implementable as specified — the adapter can at most document the requirement and ask the user to attest, which is a materially weaker guarantee than the threat model claims.
findings.evidence: voyageai.com/tos §3 + docs.voyageai.com FAQ (opt-out = dashboard toggle, admin + payment method, pre-opt-out data still trainable, irreversible) vs DECISIONS.md D-23 'PRIVACY GATE (must ship): the Voyage cloud adapter MUST enforce + document Voyage's training opt-out', CONSTRAINTS.md C-12, RISKS.md SR-2 signal 'Adapter refuses cloud without opt-out confirmed', ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §13/§15
findings.proposed_fix: Restate the invariant honestly: the adapter cannot enforce an account-level setting. Specify the real mechanism — a documented setup gate that (a) blocks the Voyage adapter until the user explicitly attests they have toggled org-level opt-out, (b) warns that any code embedded before opt-out is permanently retained for training, (c) surfaces that opt-in is irreversible. Consider whether the privacy north star permits a cloud embedder whose opt-out is un-verifiable at all, vs restricting cloud to generation-only (ZDR) with local-only embedding as the hard default. Owner must confirm the weakened guarantee.

findings.finding: The 'hybrid posture (cloud generation over ZDR + redaction)' privacy stance (D-23, C-12, §13) assumes Anthropic Zero Data Retention is available to the user. It is not by default: ZDR requires a separately-negotiated enterprise agreement with Anthropic, is not on the standard Enterprise plan, and cannot be toggled from settings. The product's actual target (D-5: 'one OS user, one machine', a local-first personal/portfolio tool) will almost never hold a ZDR agreement — so the default cloud-generation path silently runs WITHOUT ZDR, contradicting the stated posture. Compounding this: the bundled claude-api guidance defaults generation to Opus 4.8 / Fable 5, and Fable-5/Mythos-class models are NOT available under ZDR at all (mandatory 30-day retention) — so any ZDR + Fable-5 combination is structurally impossible (returns 400).
findings.evidence: privacy.claude.com + code.claude.com/docs/zero-data-retention (ZDR = separate agreement, not default, not admin-toggleable) + Anthropic Fable-5 30-day-retention carve-out (2026-06-09, not available under ZDR) vs DECISIONS.md D-23 'cloud generation over ZDR', CONSTRAINTS.md C-12 'Cloud generation over ZDR', ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §13
findings.proposed_fix: Separate aspiration from default. State that ZDR is an enterprise-only opt-in the typical single-user will not have; make the DEFAULT cloud-generation path explicit about non-ZDR retention (or make strict-local generation the default and cloud generation an informed opt-in with a clear retention warning). Pin generation to a ZDR-eligible model when ZDR is configured (Opus 4.8 is ZDR-eligible; Fable-5/Mythos-class are not) and refuse Fable-5 under a ZDR profile. Owner must confirm the default generation posture given that ZDR is not realistically available to the target user.

findings.finding: No cost model exists anywhere in the planning set despite production-grade posture. The Claude agent loop is the dominant recurring cost and is completely unquantified: Opus 4.8 is $5/$25 per 1M in/out (Fable-5 $10/$50), the agentic-RAG path is explicitly 3-10x more tokens (D-13/§8), it does whole-file hydration into a 200K-1M window, and the ≤200K-token long-context routing rule 'stuffs the whole project into context' per query — each of which can be tens-of-thousands to >200K input tokens billed per question. The Voyage cloud path is also unpriced (voyage-code-3 $0.18/M, rerank-2.5 $0.05/M, context-3 $0.18/M, 200M free tokens/mo each; re-embedding a portfolio on every model/dim change per D-14 blue-green is a bulk cost). A user can run up a large, surprising bill with no budget guard, cost telemetry, or routing-cost contract specified.
findings.evidence: claude-api skill models table (Opus 4.8 $5/$25, Fable-5 $10/$50 per 1M) + RESEARCH.md R5 'Tradeoff: 3-10x more tokens' + DECISIONS.md D-13 (long-context routing, whole-file hydration) + docs.voyageai.com/docs/pricing ($0.18 / $0.05 / $0.18 per M; 200M free/mo) vs absence of any cost line in CONSTRAINTS.md, RISKS.md, EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md, ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md
findings.proposed_fix: Add a cost contract: a per-query token/cost budget (the long-context-vs-RAG routing already has the hook — make the ≤200K threshold cost-aware, and gate the agentic loop on a budget rule as RESEARCH.md R5 already recommends 'a budget rule'), prompt-cache the stable prefix (pinned corpus/tool schemas — cache-read is 0.1x) to cut whole-file-hydration cost, surface estimated cost in the UI before an expensive agentic/long-context call, and add a cost/token eval to the harness (D-10). Add a RISKS entry for runaway API spend.

findings.finding: The default LOCAL embedder qwen3-embedding-4b (D-23/A-2, marked Apache-2.0) carries an unresolved legal ambiguity the planning set does not flag: the Qwen3-Embedding models were fine-tuned on MS MARCO, which is licensed non-commercial-only, and an open Qwen GitHub issue (#166, Nov 2025) explicitly asks for clarification of whether the Apache-2.0 weights are actually clean for commercial use — Alibaba has not definitively resolved it, and other vendors (IBM Granite) deliberately avoid MS MARCO for exactly this reason. CONSTRAINTS C-11 flags jina/zerank CC-BY-NC but misses this one, which sits on the *default* path.
findings.evidence: github.com/QwenLM/Qwen3-Embedding/issues/166 (MS MARCO non-commercial fine-tuning data, unresolved commercial-use clarification) vs DECISIONS.md D-23 'qwen3-embedding-4b (Apache-2.0)', ASSUMPTIONS.md A-2 [VERIFIED], CONSTRAINTS.md C-11 (flags jina/zerank, not qwen3/MS-MARCO)
findings.proposed_fix: Add the MS MARCO caveat to C-11 / the provider menu (D-23) as a known-ambiguity (license downgraded from clean [VERIFIED] Apache-2.0 to 'Apache-2.0 weights, MS MARCO fine-tuning data non-commercial — commercial-use clarity pending upstream'). Track Qwen issue #166. Keep bge-m3 (MIT, no MS MARCO) registered as the clean-license fallback default for users who need a commercially-unambiguous local embedder. Note for the owner that this is a personal/portfolio tool so 'commercial use' may not bite — but the [VERIFIED] tag overstates certainty.

findings.finding: The FastMCP 3.0 breaking-change budget (D-24/C-8/TR-4) is understated. D-24 lists only 'ui=→app=, removed kwargs, decorators no longer wrap', but FastMCP 3.0 GA (2026-02-18, now at 3.4.1) also: removed 16 FastMCP() constructor kwargs (not just 'some'), re-scoped background tasks from MCP-session to authorization-context (a semantic change for any session-scoped state — relevant to the stdio trust-boundary server), and removed RouteType / FastMCPOpenAPI / get_prompts() in 3.0.2, with further breaking changes through 3.4.x. 'Pin the major' (D-24) is insufficient given breaking changes WITHIN the 3.x line.
findings.evidence: github.com/PrefectHQ/fastmcp/releases + gofastmcp.com/updates (3.0 GA 2026-02-18; 16 kwargs removed; background-task scoping change; RouteType/FastMCPOpenAPI/get_prompts removed in 3.0.2; 3.4.x breaking changes) vs DECISIONS.md D-24, CONSTRAINTS.md C-8 'pinned major', RISKS.md TR-4
findings.proposed_fix: Pin an exact minor/patch (e.g. `fastmcp==3.4.x`) not just the major, given breaking changes within 3.x; add a conformance test against the pinned version (TR-4 already wants this). Note the background-task auth-context scoping change as a thing to verify against the loopback-token/stdio trust model (D-5), since the MCP server is the trust boundary.

findings.finding: The reranker/embedder bake-off (O-BAKEOFF) names cloud candidates whose licensing the user should confirm before the bake-off, not after: zerank-2 weights are CC-BY-NC-4.0 (commercial deployment requires a paid ZeroEntropy contract / AWS-Azure Marketplace SKU; usable only via paid API at $0.025/M for commercial) and cohere-rerank-4-pro is closed/API-only. CONSTRAINTS C-11 correctly flags zerank-2 as API-only, but the bake-off (O-BAKEOFF) and D-23 present them as registered candidates without restating that 'winning' the bake-off still means an ongoing paid-API dependency (and per-query cost) for a tool whose whole thesis is local-first. This is a posture question, not a fact error.
findings.evidence: huggingface.co/zeroentropy/zerank-2-reranker (CC-BY-NC-4.0, commercial = paid contract) + zeroentropy docs ($0.025/M API) vs DECISIONS.md D-23 / 'Still OPEN' O-BAKEOFF, CONSTRAINTS.md C-11
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm with owner: is an ongoing paid cloud-reranker API dependency acceptable for the local-first product, or should the bake-off be scoped to permissively-licensed self-hostable rerankers (qwen3-reranker Apache-2.0 is already the local default; mxbai-rerank-large-v2) with cloud rerankers explicitly opt-in + cost-flagged? If cloud rerankers stay in scope, restate that a winning CC-BY-NC model is API-only (no self-host) and add its per-query cost to the cost model (D6-4).

dimension: D7 — Inconsistent or unlocked decisions; internal contradictions across the 18 artifacts
summary: The decision set is unusually coherent and well-cross-referenced, but D7 surfaces one load-bearing status contradiction that must be resolved before finalize: D-20, D-21, D-22 are tagged [proposed] in DECISIONS.md, yet CLAUDE_CODE_HANDOFF, OPEN_QUESTIONS, ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT, CONSTRAINTS, REQUIREMENTS, RISKS, and ASSUMPTIONS all treat them as locked/decided ground truth. These three govern packaging/distribution, the entire core/host-adapter+monorepo+published-core topology, and the ship-instrumented-but-silent observability posture — all load-bearing, all already wired into the draft as [LOCKED]. They should either be locked by the owner or every downstream "locked" reference re-tagged. Secondary: three upstream source docs (PRD, ONBOARDING) still carry the pre-decision "npm i -g / service / [OPEN] Python-vs-Node" framing that D-17/D-20/D-21 explicitly supersede; the doc-rename + framing pass flagged in the handoff has not been run. Minor cosmetic status-tag inconsistencies (D-12/D-13 lack the owner sub-tag; a known ResourceType count gap is correctly flagged as cross-doc).

findings.finding: D-20 (Distribution/packaging), D-21 (one-core/two-adapters + monorepo + published core), and D-22 (ship instrumented-but-silent observability) are tagged `[proposed]` in DECISIONS.md, but every other artifact treats them as already locked. CLAUDE_CODE_HANDOFF §'Decisions locked' line 18-19 lists 'D-1..D-25' as locked and explicitly enumerates 'packaging (dmg/brew/sidecar), one-core/two-adapters/monorepo+published-core, ship-instrumented-but-silent'. OPEN_QUESTIONS line 20 lists 'packaging (D-20), repo/separation (D-21), ship-obs (D-22)' under 'Decided (formerly open)'. ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §3 lists 'ports-and-adapters core (D-21)' as part of the `[LOCKED]` spine; §14 tags ship-instrumented-but-silent `[PH]`/`[LOCKED]`; §16 packaging `[LOCKED]`. These three are load-bearing (they fix the build's distribution channel, the entire module/repo topology that §6 builds on, and the privacy/telemetry posture). A [proposed] tag means the owner has not confirmed — finalize cannot treat them as binding while the source-of-truth ledger says they are not.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-20/D-21/D-22 (all `[proposed]`) vs docs/planning/CLAUDE_CODE_HANDOFF.md:18-19, docs/planning/OPEN_QUESTIONS.md:20, docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§3/§14/§16, docs/planning/CONSTRAINTS.md:C-2/C-10/C-13/C-15, docs/planning/REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-21/PH-6
findings.proposed_fix: Owner confirms D-20, D-21, D-22 → re-tag to `[locked — owner]` in DECISIONS.md. If the owner is NOT ready to lock any of them, instead downgrade the corresponding [LOCKED] tags in ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §3/§6/§14/§16 and remove them from the OPEN_QUESTIONS 'Decided' list + the handoff 'Decisions locked' list, so a single source of truth holds. Do not let finalize bind a [proposed] decision silently.

findings.finding: The ONBOARDING_AND_DOC_LIFECYCLE companion doc (a doc the finalizer is told to read) still specifies the install posture that D-20 explicitly supersedes: R-INSTALL line 9 says 'Installable as a global package / CLI (e.g. `npm i -g project-brain` or `npx project-brain`)'. D-20 states verbatim 'npm is NOT the primary channel ... supersedes the ONBOARDING doc's `npm i -g` idea' (signed/notarized .dmg + Homebrew Cask + bundled PyInstaller sidecar is the channel). The superseded doc is still live and unannotated, so a reader of ONBOARDING alone gets the wrong, contradicted install contract.
findings.evidence: docs/requirements/ONBOARDING_AND_DOC_LIFECYCLE.md:9 (R-INSTALL `npm i -g`) contradicted by docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-20 ('npm is NOT the primary ... supersedes the ONBOARDING doc')
findings.proposed_fix: Add a superseded-by-D-20 banner at the top of ONBOARDING R-INSTALL (or edit line 9 to point to the .dmg/Cask/pipx/bundled-sidecar channel), so the companion doc stops asserting the npm-global install that D-20 overrode.

findings.finding: The PRD (an upstream source doc the finalizer must read) internally contradicts the locked sidecar decision and still carries an open question that is now resolved. Line 4 calls Project Brain 'the memory/reasoning/action-planning **service**' while PRD line 1023 (ADR-005 resolution) and MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md line 36 both say to 'Drop the old service/library framing' (it is a stdio-MCP sidecar, not a service/library). PRD line 1018 still tags '[OPEN, leaning resolved] Python-first CLI, Node wrapper, or both?' — but D-20/D-17/D-21 lock Python-core + bundled sidecar + npm-not-primary, so this question is resolved, not open. The handoff (line 25) flagged that a doc-rename/framing pass to 'Nexus Brain' is pending; this is the same un-run pass leaving stale framing in the PRD.
findings.evidence: docs/product/PRD.md:4 ('service') and PRD:1018 ('[OPEN] Python-first CLI, Node wrapper, or both?') vs docs/product/PRD.md:1023 ('Replaces the old ... framing'), docs/integrations/MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:36 ('Drop the old service/library framing'), docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-17/D-20/D-21; pending pass noted at docs/planning/CLAUDE_CODE_HANDOFF.md:25
findings.proposed_fix: Run the deferred framing/rename pass on the PRD: change line 4 'service' to 'engine/sidecar', and resolve PRD line 1018's [OPEN] tag to RESOLVED pointing at D-17/D-20/D-21 (mirroring how line 1023 was already resolved). Or, if the pass is intentionally deferred to post-finalize, the finalizer should state that the PRD's 'service'/[OPEN] wording is stale and DECISIONS D-17/D-20/D-21 govern.

findings.finding: Status-tag inconsistency within DECISIONS.md itself: D-12 (three-store model) and D-13 (RAG pipeline) are tagged plain `[locked]` (no '— owner' sub-tag), whereas the surrounding load-bearing decisions D-2..D-11, D-14..D-19 carry `[locked — owner]`. D-15 is also plain `[locked]`. The ledger header defines only `locked / proposed / open` as statuses, so '[locked]' vs '[locked — owner]' is an undocumented sub-distinction. Either both are genuinely owner-confirmed (then tag them `[locked — owner]`) or the plain-`[locked]` ones were locked by Brain-1 inference and the owner has not separately confirmed the three-store map / RAG pipeline / redaction model — which would make them weaker than their neighbors despite being equally load-bearing.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-12, D-13, D-15 (plain `[locked]`) vs D-2..D-11/D-14/D-16..D-19 (`[locked — owner]`); header line 3 defines no `[locked]` vs `[locked — owner]` distinction
findings.proposed_fix: Define the '— owner' sub-tag in the DECISIONS header (owner-confirmed vs Brain-1-locked-by-inference), then normalize D-12/D-13/D-15 to whichever is true. If they are owner-confirmed, add '— owner'; if not, surface them to the owner for explicit confirmation since they are load-bearing.

findings.finding: Transport-primacy framing differs between D-5 (standalone trust posture) and D-17 (desktop app). D-5 says the PRIMARY transport is 'stdio MCP (no port)' with loopback HTTP as the 'optional opt-in' fallback. D-17 says the Tauri shell talks to the Python core 'over loopback HTTP + per-launch token (D-5) or stdio', and ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §4.1 shows the desktop↔core link as 'loopback HTTP + per-launch token (D-5) OR in-process' while reserving 'stdio MCP' for external agents. This is reconcilable (the desktop-shell↔core link is a different channel than the external-agent MCP link), but the docs never state which transport is primary for the in-app shell↔core link, and the integrated NexusOps path (ADR-005) makes stdio primary with loopback as the notarization fallback. Left implicit, /tasks-gen could pick either transport for the standalone shell↔core seam.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-5 ('Primary transport = stdio MCP ... Optional opt-in loopback HTTP') vs D-17 ('loopback HTTP + per-launch token (D-5) or stdio') and docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§4.1 (desktop↔core = 'loopback HTTP ... OR in-process'); integrated path docs/integrations/MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:36 (stdio primary, loopback fallback)
findings.proposed_fix: Owner/finalizer should state the primary transport for the standalone Tauri-shell↔Python-core link explicitly (in-process embedded vs loopback HTTP vs stdio) and note it is distinct from the external-agent MCP channel, so the shell↔core seam is one decision rather than an implied either/or.

findings.finding: The ResourceType / IdKind count gap is already correctly flagged as a known cross-doc gap in MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md line 149 ('code carries ~20; ARCH §6.2/Appendix A name 21'; 22 frozen IdKind). Nexus Brain's DOMAIN_MODEL §states 'Shared IDs map 1:1 to NexusOps's 22 IdKind' (ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §5). This is consistent on the 22-IdKind front, but the Brain docs do not separately track the platform-side ResourceType drift (20 vs 21), so if Brain ever maps EvidenceRef/object_refs to ResourceType it inherits an unresolved count. Not a Brain-internal contradiction — a forward seam dependency flagged for awareness.
findings.evidence: docs/integrations/MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:149 (ResourceType 20-vs-21 gap, 'known cross-doc gap'), docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§5 ('22 IdKind', 1:1)
findings.proposed_fix: No Brain-side action required now; finalizer should carry the platform-side ResourceType count as an open seam item (resolve when NexusOps Phase 8 freezes BrainEventMapping) and not bind a specific ResourceType count in ARCHITECTURE.md.

dimension: D8/D8a — SCOPE (production-grade): overbuilt vs under-built production concerns
summary: Against the agreed production-grade scope, the draft is NOT meaningfully overbuilt — federation-from-day-one (D-2), the HostPort/NexusOps seam-from-day-one (D-21), and the dual Langfuse+SigNoz backends are each owner-locked or explicitly dev/CI-only and defensible. The real exposure is UNDER-BUILD (D8a). Seven production concerns the posture declares "baseline, not deferrable" (CONSTRAINTS C-17) are missing or underspecified: (1) MCP-boundary INPUT validation — the draft treats the MCP server only as an egress redaction boundary and never specifies ingress validation (path-traversal in get_file, project_id scoping, query/result size caps, DoS); (2) the loopback HTTP token's authn lifecycle (entropy, storage, handoff to the UI, expiry, constant-time compare, 127.0.0.1 binding) is named but never specified; (3) structured application logging + log-redaction is entirely absent (only off-by-default OTel tracing exists); (4) no migration path for the manifest.json schemaVersion or the global registry schema — "exclude + flag on mismatch" forces a full re-index, and OQ-6 leaves the manifest schema unfrozen; (5) add-idempotency (re-adding the same repo) is implied but not stated; (6) the Tauri auto-updater has no rollback/downgrade story and no app↔sidecar↔store-schema version-compatibility contract across an update. These are load-bearing because they change the MCP tool contract, the manifest/registry schema contract, and the deploy/rollback contract the owner must confirm.

findings.finding: MCP-boundary INPUT validation is missing. Every artifact frames the MCP server purely as an EGRESS trust boundary — 'redact + policy-filter before results leave' (ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §11; THREAT_MODEL T-6; FR-13). Nothing specifies INGRESS validation of tool inputs: the tools search/get_file/graph/list_projects/status take caller-supplied arguments, but there is no contract for validating/sanitizing them. get_file with a caller path is a path-traversal vector (../, absolute paths, symlinks escaping the repo / project_id scope); search/graph have no query-size, result-count, or token caps (DoS / cost-amplification, especially via the always-on loopback-HTTP transport which any local process can reach); project_id is not validated as belonging to the registry before fan-out. FastMCP/Pydantic gives type validation for free, but the semantic/security validation (path containment, scope authorization, bounds) is unspecified and is exactly the kind of production correctness C-17 calls baseline.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§11 (MCP server = trust boundary, egress-only framing); docs/planning/THREAT_MODEL.md:T-6 (only redact+policy-filter on the way OUT); docs/planning/REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-12/FR-13 (no input-validation requirement)
findings.proposed_fix: Add an explicit MCP-ingress validation contract to §11 (and an FR): (a) get_file/graph resolve+canonicalize the requested path and assert it is contained within the requested project's source root (reject .., absolute, symlink-escape); (b) project_id/scope must resolve to a registry entry the policy.yaml permits before any fan-out, else omit-with-marker (consistent with Flow 4); (c) bound query length, k/result-count, and response token size with documented limits and a clear over-limit error; (d) state that FastMCP tool args are Pydantic-validated AND that semantic validation above runs before any store access. Pin this as a test-first item alongside redaction.

findings.finding: The loopback-HTTP authn token is named but its lifecycle is unspecified. D-5/C-4/D-USR-2 repeatedly say 'per-launch token' on 127.0.0.1, but no artifact states how the token is generated (CSPRNG, length), where it is stored, how the Tauri UI / a browser client obtains it without leaking it (env var? file with 0600? handshake?), whether it expires or rotates within a long-lived session, that comparison is constant-time, and that the listener binds 127.0.0.1 (not 0.0.0.0). For a 'production-grade' security posture where the loopback path is the standalone UI's primary transport (D-17), this is the one authn mechanism in the system and it is left as a phrase.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-5; docs/planning/CONSTRAINTS.md:C-4; docs/planning/USERS.md:D-USR-2; docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§4.1/§11 (token mentioned, never specified)
findings.proposed_fix: Specify the token contract in §11/§15: CSPRNG ≥128-bit, minted per sidecar launch, handed to the Tauri shell over the spawn channel (never written world-readable), required on every loopback request via header, constant-time compared, listener bound to 127.0.0.1 only; define behavior on token-absent/mismatch (401, no body). State whether a long-lived UI session re-mints on sidecar recycle (D-25 W5 recycles the sidecar — the token contract must cover that).

findings.finding: Structured application logging is absent. The observability story is OTel tracing only, and it ships OFF by default + local-only (D-9/D-22/PH-6). That leaves no specified always-on structured application log for an end-user product — the very thing needed to diagnose a failed setup/add/sync on a user's machine when tracing is off. There is also no statement that ordinary logs are redacted: THREAT_MODEL T-3 forbids secrets 'in config, the index, events, or traces' but conspicuously does NOT list logs, and the Redactor is only specified at the embed/MCP-egress boundaries — so a stack trace or error log could echo a secret-bearing line. C-17 lists 'observability' as baseline; structured logging is the floor of that.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-9/D-22 (OTel off-by-default, local-only); docs/planning/REQUIREMENTS.md:PH-6; docs/planning/THREAT_MODEL.md:T-3 (logs omitted from the secret-exclusion list); docs/planning/CONSTRAINTS.md:C-17
findings.proposed_fix: Add a structured-logging contract (§14 or §15): always-on JSON logs with levels, written local-only to a known path, rotated; the Redactor (or a log filter) scrubs secret-shaped tokens from log payloads before write; explicitly extend T-3's exclusion list to include logs; clarify the OTel toggle is for deep tracing, not a substitute for app logs. State that logs never phone home (consistent with D-22).

findings.finding: No migration path for the local-store / manifest / registry schema versions. The architecture covers the index-as-cache rebuild path well (blue-green re-embed on model/dim change, atomic swap, rollback — D-14/PH-5/Flow 9) and gates reads on schema_version mismatch by 'exclude + flag' (§18; Flow 5). But schema_version belongs to data that is NOT a derived cache: the .project-brain/manifest.json (schemaVersion) and the global ~/.project-brain/registry. When the BRAIN's own manifest/registry schema bumps across an app update, 'exclude + flag / rebuild from source' means every project silently re-indexes (potentially expensive cloud re-embeds), and there is no defined migrator. OQ-6 explicitly leaves the manifest schema unfrozen ('freeze at first ingest slice'). C-17 names 'the update/migration path for the local stores + manifest schema versions' as baseline; it is unaddressed.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DATA_MODEL.md:§③ (manifest schemaVersion + registry schema, no migration); docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§18 ('Schema/model mismatch → exclude + flag' — rebuild, not migrate); docs/planning/OPEN_QUESTIONS.md:OQ-6 (manifest schema unfrozen); docs/planning/CONSTRAINTS.md:C-17
findings.proposed_fix: Add a schema-migration contract distinct from index rebuild: version the manifest and registry schemas explicitly; on app/sidecar upgrade run forward migrators that transform manifest/registry in place WITHOUT forcing a full re-embed when only catalog metadata changed; reserve 'rebuild from source' for true index-format breaks. Define what happens when a NEWER store is opened by an OLDER binary (refuse, don't corrupt). Promote OQ-6 to a freeze-before-first-ingest gate.

findings.finding: Auto-updater rollback and app↔sidecar↔store version-compatibility are unspecified. D-20/§16 ship a Tauri auto-updater (signed/notarized dmg + Homebrew Cask), but there is no rollback/downgrade story if a bad version ships, and no statement that an app update which bumps the bundled PyInstaller sidecar (and possibly the manifest/index schema) is coordinated — i.e., what guarantees the new sidecar can open stores written by the prior one, or that a downgraded app can still read a forward-migrated store (ties to D8a-4). For a no-timebox production-grade desktop product with a self-updating channel, deploy/rollback (C-17) is half-specified: forward install is covered, recovery from a bad release is not.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-20 (auto-updater, no rollback); docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§16 (notarization/install only); docs/planning/CONSTRAINTS.md:C-17 (deploy/rollback baseline)
findings.proposed_fix: Specify a release-safety contract in §16: how a user rolls back a bad app/sidecar version (keep N-1 installable; Homebrew Cask pin path); a compatibility matrix asserting sidecar vN reads store schemas ≥ vK and refuses unknown-newer; tie the updater to the D8a-4 migration gate so an update runs migrations transactionally and a downgrade fails safe (refuse) rather than corrupting. Note the brew-cask vs in-app-updater dual channel needs one source of truth for 'current version'.

findings.finding: Idempotency of `add` (re-adding the same repo) is implied but never stated. setup, sync, and re-embed have explicit idempotency contracts (FR-19, PH-4, Flow 9). But Flow 2 `add <repo>` does not state what happens on a second `add` of an already-registered repo: does it no-op, refresh, error, or duplicate a registry row / second LanceDB dataset? Given add writes the registry row, the manifest, enrolls the watcher, and inits CodeGraph, a non-idempotent re-add could double-enroll watchers/hooks or orphan a dataset. C-17 lists idempotency of setup/sync/re-index as baseline; add sits squarely in that set.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 2 (no re-add semantics); docs/planning/REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-19 (idempotency named for setup/host-config, not add); docs/planning/CONSTRAINTS.md:C-17
findings.proposed_fix: State in Flow 2 / FR-1 that `add` is idempotent: re-adding a registered repo updates (not duplicates) the registry row + manifest, re-enrolls watcher/hook idempotently, and either refreshes or no-ops the index based on the source SHA — never creates a second dataset or double-installs the git hook.

findings.finding: Overbuild check — borderline item to confirm, not a defect. Building the full HostPort + NexusOpsHost seam 'from day one' (D-21, ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §12) while NexusOps Phase 8 is deferred/unbuilt (MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE build-state caveat) is the only candidate for 'beyond agreed scope.' I judge it JUSTIFIED, not overbuilt: D-4/D-USR-1 already force the standalone bounded-allowlist to route through HostPort, so the port earns its keep for the shipping standalone product; only the NexusOpsHost *adapter* is deferred (P2). The dual Langfuse+SigNoz stack is dev/CI-only (D-22/C-10) and not shipped, so not user-facing overbuild. Federation-from-day-one (D-2) and the 12-state WorkflowInstance machine (P1/P2) are owner-locked / alignment-frozen. So: no overbuild to cut — flagging only so the owner confirms the HostPort-from-day-one cost is accepted given Phase 8 is unbuilt.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-21/D-4; docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§12; docs/integrations/MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:build-state caveat (Phase 8 deferred/unbuilt); docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:D-22/C-10 (obs backends not shipped)
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm with the owner that building the HostPort seam now (not just the StandaloneHost behind it) is accepted scope; if the only near-term implementer is StandaloneHost, keep the port interface minimal and let the NexusOpsHost shape be co-designed at Phase 8 rather than pre-specified, to avoid speculative contract surface (IR-1 risk).

dimension: D9 — Testing strategy gaps / untestable designs
summary: The draft's testing strategy (ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §17 + EVALUATION_CRITERIA) names the right buckets (test-first deterministic vs eval-covered non-deterministic) but is underspecified at every load-bearing seam and contains a direct test-vs-eval contradiction. The most serious gaps: (1) the grounding gate — the north-star control — has no specified deterministic test seam (no way to feed a recorded Citations-API payload + fixed retrieval so the span-existence check is unit-testable without a live model); EVALUATION_CRITERIA conflates the deterministic span-check with non-deterministic generation. (2) Federation rank-fusion is classified as test-first in §17 but eval-covered (non-deterministic) in EVALUATION_CRITERIA line 34 — a contradiction that must be resolved, plus RRF tie-break determinism is unspecified. (3) The "injectable clock/seed" and "recorded fixtures" are named as phrases but never defined as architectural ports/fake types (NexusOps, by contrast, names Clock + IdGen + FakeHarness + FakePty explicitly) — so they are untestable as drawn: no FakeEmbeddingProvider/FakeReranker/FakeModelProvider seam, no record/replay mechanism named. (4) The redaction property/fuzz harness is named but has no recall-floor/FP-ceiling numbers, corpus-construction method, or generator strategy — and PRD open-question #12 "How should transcript redaction be tested?" is still [OPEN], so the draft treats a contractually-open question as settled. (5) RISKS names two contract/snapshot tests (IR-1 seam snapshot, IR-2 core-API stability) that §17 omits entirely; there is no schema-conformance test against the frozen nexusops-contract.schema.json 0.34.0 / 22 IdKind / EvidenceRef enum. (6) Golden-set construction is asserted ("versioned in-repo") but the labeling method, ground-truth source for Recall@k and for anchor-state (live/stale/moved/unknown), and dataset-size targets are absent. Several CI gates reference metrics whose thresholds are explicitly deferred to "first bake-off," leaving the release gate undefined at draft time.

findings.finding: The grounding gate — the north-star trust control (D-6/D-7) — has no specified DETERMINISTIC test seam. The gate's post-validation step ('does every cited span actually exist in current source?') is fully deterministic given a fixed retrieval result + a fixed model citation payload, yet §17 lists only 'anchor parse/revalidate' as test-first and does NOT name the gate itself. EVALUATION_CRITERIA puts 'Grounding-gate correctness → 100%' and 'Stale-anchor false-confidence rate → zero (hard gate)' under the Trust eval table (non-deterministic, model-in-the-loop), conflating the deterministic span-existence check with non-deterministic generation. There is no named seam to inject a recorded Anthropic Citations-API response so the gate logic is exercised hermetically. Result: the single most important correctness gate is, as drawn, only testable behind a live model — which makes 'zero unflagged unsupported claims' unverifiable as a hard CI gate.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§17 (test-first list omits the gate); EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md:13-18 (gate correctness placed in the non-deterministic Trust table); USER_FLOWS.md:F-GG; DECISIONS.md:D-7
findings.proposed_fix: Split the grounding gate into two tiers in ARCHITECTURE.md: (a) a DETERMINISTIC post-validation contract (span-existence + anchor-state check) tested test-first against fixed (retrieval-result, citation-payload) fixtures — a recorded Citations-API response replayed through a ModelProvider fake; assert 100% flag of injected unsupported/stale citations; (b) end-to-end gate behavior under live generation covered by the eval suite. Define the recorded-citation fixture format and the ModelProvider record/replay seam (see D9-3).

findings.finding: Direct contradiction on federation rank-fusion classification. ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §17 lists 'federation rank-fusion' under Test-first (deterministic). EVALUATION_CRITERIA line 34 lists 'federation ranking' under 'Non-deterministic surfaces (eval-covered, not unit-tested)'. These cannot both be true. Additionally, RRF determinism is unspecified: with union over N per-project result sets, tie-breaking (equal RRF scores across repos), input ordering stability, and float-accumulation order all affect the final ranking — none is pinned, so even the 'deterministic' reading is not yet a reproducible test.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§17 (federation rank-fusion = test-first) vs EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md:34 (federation ranking = eval-covered); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§9; DATA_MODEL.md:60-61
findings.proposed_fix: Resolve to: RRF FUSION MATH is deterministic and test-first (fixed per-repo ranked lists in → one fused ranking out, with a specified total-order tie-break, e.g. (rrf_score desc, project_id asc, chunk_id asc) and a defined float-accumulation order); RETRIEVAL QUALITY of the fused result (does the right cross-repo chunk surface) is eval-covered. State the tie-break rule as a binding invariant so the unit test is reproducible across runs/platforms.

findings.finding: The 'injectable clock/seed' and 'recorded fixtures' that the whole non-deterministic test strategy rests on are named as phrases but never defined as architectural seams. There is no Clock port, no Seed/IdGen port, and no FakeEmbeddingProvider / FakeReranker / FakeModelProvider / FakeContextStrategy type in §6's port list or anywhere in the artifacts — contrast the sibling NexusOps, which explicitly names an injectable Clock + IdGen plus FakeHarness + FakePty (NexusOps CLAUDE.md 'TDD posture'; ARCHITECTURE §14). The provider registry (D-23) lists pluggable providers but never a test/fake adapter or a record/replay mechanism. As drawn, the agent loop, retrieval, and re-embed paths have no hermetic test substrate, so 'recorded fixtures + injectable clock/seed' is aspirational, not buildable.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§4.3/§6 (port list: HostPort, EventSource, EmbeddingProvider/Reranker/ContextStrategy/ModelProvider, ObservabilitySink, SecretStore — no Clock, no Seed/IdGen, no Fake* test seam); EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md:11,34; cf. NexusOps CLAUDE.md 'TDD posture' (Clock+IdGen, FakeHarness, FakePty)
findings.proposed_fix: Add to the ports list and ARCHITECTURE.md: a Clock port and a Seed/IdGen port (both injectable, mirroring NexusOps) threaded through anchor revalidation, drift-radar ranking (authority×recency uses time), manifest timestamps, and any RNG in chunking/sampling. Add named test doubles — FakeEmbeddingProvider/FakeReranker/FakeModelProvider (deterministic vectors/scores), and a record/replay adapter (cassette-style) for cloud providers + the Citations API. Name the fixture storage location and format. Make these first-class so the eval/fixture path is implementable.

findings.finding: The redaction property/fuzz harness is named as a day-0 spike and a merge-blocking CI gate ('redaction-recall fuzz=0'), but its specifics are entirely absent: no recall-FLOOR or false-positive CEILING numbers, no corpus-construction method, no fuzz generator / mutation strategy, and no definition of 'a leak' (into index? into cloud payload? both?). THREAT_MODEL line 27 even says the harness has 'a recall-floor / FP-ceiling' — but no value is given anywhere. Worse, the PRD lists open-question #12 'How should transcript redaction be tested?' as still [OPEN], so the draft asserts a settled hard gate over a contractually-open question. A hard merge gate with no specified threshold or generator is untestable as written.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§17 (redaction-recall fuzz=0, merge gate) & §19 (day-0 spike); EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md:23; THREAT_MODEL.md:26-27 (asserts a floor/ceiling, gives no number); PRD.md:1026 (open-question #12 still [OPEN])
findings.proposed_fix: Specify the harness in ARCHITECTURE.md: (a) leak definition = any secret-shaped token surviving into either the LanceDB chunk text/vector OR the cloud-egress payload OR an MCP-boundary result; (b) a property-based generator (token-prefix set from THREAT_MODEL × placement contexts × entropy bands) plus a curated/adversarial seed corpus as a living regression fixture; (c) a quantified recall floor (e.g. 100% on the curated prefix set; ≥X% on entropy-class fuzz) and an explicit FP ceiling so the entropy fallback doesn't over-quarantine; (d) resolve PRD OQ#12 or flag it as a question-for-human blocking the gate. Note the redactor runs at THREE points (persist/embed, cloud-egress, MCP boundary) — the harness must assert all three.

findings.finding: RISKS names two contract/snapshot tests as the mitigations for the NexusOps-seam and core-API risks — IR-1 'Contract snapshot test at the seam' and IR-2 'Core API stability test' — but §17's testing strategy lists NEITHER. There is no schema-conformance test asserting the core's emitted ActionPlan/EvidenceRef/IdKind shapes match the frozen nexusops-contract.schema.json (CONTRACT_VERSION 0.34.0), the 22 frozen IdKind, the 11-value EvidenceType enum, or the EventEnvelope the redacted outbox delivers. The seam itself flags a known drift (ResourceType count: code ~20 vs ARCH §6.2/Appendix A name 21) — exactly the class of mismatch a snapshot/contract test exists to catch — yet no such test is in the strategy.
findings.evidence: RISKS.md:23-24 (IR-1 contract snapshot test, IR-2 core-API stability test) vs ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§17 (neither present); MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:45,55,78,149 (frozen 22 IdKind, 11-value EvidenceType, ResourceType count gap); PRD.md:1020,1027 (frozen ActionPlan family + EventEnvelope schemas)
findings.proposed_fix: Add to ARCHITECTURE.md §17: (a) a contract/snapshot test that validates the published nexus-brain-core serialized shapes (ActionPlan/ActionPlanStep/ActionRequest, EvidenceRef, status report, the 22 IdKind prefixes) against the pinned nexusops-contract.schema.json 0.34.0 (generate Pydantic from the JSON-Schema per PRD #6 and snapshot-assert round-trip); (b) a core-public-API stability/snapshot test guarding the standalone↔embedded contract (D-21). Even with NexusOpsHost deferred [P2], the serialization conformance is testable from day one against frozen primitives.

findings.finding: Golden-set CONSTRUCTION is asserted but never specified. EVALUATION_CRITERIA says datasets are 'versioned in-repo' and lists Recall@k and anchor-revalidation accuracy as metrics 'vs ground truth' — but never says HOW ground truth is produced: who/what labels the Q→relevant-chunk relevance set (manual? LLM-bootstrapped? mined from existing docs?), how the anchor-state ground truth (live/stale/moved/unknown) corpus is constructed (this needs deliberately-mutated code at known SHAs to be a real test), how big the golden set is for the trust gate (the bake-off sets are 100–500 queries, but the in-repo CI golden set has no size target), or how dataset versioning interacts with the no-regression release gate when the golden set itself grows. Without this, the release gate 'eval no-regression' has no reproducible baseline.
findings.evidence: EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md:8-12,19-20,33-37; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§14 (golden sets in-repo) & §17 (eval no-regression = release gate); DECISIONS.md:D-10
findings.proposed_fix: Specify golden-set construction in EVALUATION_CRITERIA/ARCHITECTURE.md: the labeling source + review process for Q→relevant-chunk relevance; an anchor-state golden corpus built from a fixture repo with scripted edits at known git SHAs (so live/stale/moved/unknown are mechanically ground-truthed and the revalidator is testable end-to-end); a minimum CI golden-set size distinct from the larger bake-off sets; and a dataset-versioning rule defining how 'no-regression' is computed when the set changes (pin a baseline dataset version per release).

findings.finding: Multiple CI release gates reference metrics whose thresholds are explicitly deferred to 'first bake-off', leaving the gate undefined. EVALUATION_CRITERIA: 'thresholds set at first bake-off'; the Perf row ('within budget (O-LANCE-BAKEOFF)') and all retrieval/rerank targets ('high', 'maximize') are directional, not numeric. The maintenance-contract 'invisibility' acceptance (O-LANCE-BAKEOFF, TR-2) has no pass/fail latency/RAM/disk budget. So 'eval no-regression (release)' can gate on regression-vs-previous, but the absolute trust/retrieval/perf bars are unspecified, and a first release has no prior baseline to regress against. The maintenance-contract scheduling logic is listed test-first (§17) but its observable SLO (optimize latency ceiling, num_unindexed_rows lag bound) is unquantified, so the test can only assert 'it ran', not 'it stayed invisible'.
findings.evidence: EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md:13,25,30 (thresholds deferred; perf 'within budget'); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§17 (maintenance-contract scheduling = test-first) & §7 (num_unindexed_rows≈0); RISKS.md:8 (TR-2 signal = O-LANCE-BAKEOFF measures, no budget); ASSUMPTIONS.md:8 (A-4 must-verify)
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) set provisional numeric floors now (citation precision floor, Recall@k floor, optimize-latency ceiling, num_unindexed_rows staleness bound, index-build RAM cap) and mark them 'ratify at first bake-off', or (b) explicitly state in ARCHITECTURE.md that the FIRST release establishes the baseline and define the absolute hard gates that DON'T wait on bake-off (the zero-leak redaction gate, zero stale-anchor-false-confidence, zero unauthorized-mutation, no-egress) vs the comparative gates that do. Quantify the maintenance-contract invisibility SLO so its scheduling test asserts a budget, not just execution.

findings.finding: No test strategy is named for the degradation/recovery matrix or for idempotency — both declared production-grade baseline (CONSTRAINTS C-17, PH-4, PH-9) and rich with deterministic, testable failure paths. USER_FLOWS F9/F10 specify precise behaviors (crash mid-reindex → prior generation still serves; idempotent delta resume from content-hash manifest; corrupt store → rebuild from source; schema/model mismatch → exclude+flag; model API down → retrieval-only). §17 lists 'state machines' as test-first but never lists crash-injection / idempotency / atomic-swap / blue-green-rollback tests, even though these are deterministic and are the spine of the trust-on-failure story. TR-5 names a 'crash-recovery + re-embed-equivalence test' that §17 omits.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§17 (no crash/idempotency/recovery tests) & §18; USER_FLOWS.md:F9-F10; REQUIREMENTS.md:PH-4,PH-9 (NFR-2 reproducibility); RISKS.md:11 (TR-5 crash-recovery + re-embed-equivalence test); CONSTRAINTS.md:C-17
findings.proposed_fix: Add a Failure/Recovery test class to §17: crash-injection mid-reindex asserting the prior generation still serves and delta-resume is idempotent (run twice → identical index, the re-embed-equivalence test from TR-5); atomic-swap/blue-green rollback tests; corrupt-store → rebuild-from-source reproducibility test (NFR-2: same source+stamp → byte/score-equivalent index); schema/model-mismatch exclude+flag test; and a degraded-mode test per F10 branch (model down → retrieval-only; CodeGraph down → tree-sitter/CLI fallback).

findings.finding: The MCP-boundary trust filter — declared THE trust boundary (D-3/§11, T-6) — has no dedicated boundary/contract test in §17. The boundary must redact + policy-filter results regardless of caller and honor per-project policy.yaml (F4: policy-denied project → omitted-with-marker, not hard error). This is deterministic (given fixed retrieval results + a policy fixture, the filtered output is fixed) and security-load-bearing, yet §17 folds it under generic 'redaction' and never asserts the policy-filter / marker behavior or that the same redactor fires on the egress path. The loopback-HTTP per-launch-token auth (D-5) is also untested in the strategy.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§11 & §17; THREAT_MODEL.md:18 (T-6); USER_FLOWS.md:F4 (policy-denied → omitted-with-marker); REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-13; DECISIONS.md:D-5
findings.proposed_fix: Add an MCP-boundary contract test: fixed retrieval result + policy.yaml fixture → assert redaction applied, denied projects omitted-with-marker (not error), and stable-ID/file:line shape of structured results; assert the egress redactor is the SAME engine as the embed redactor (one code path, T-1/T-6 continuity). Add a loopback-token auth test (valid/invalid/missing token; no-open-port-by-default assertion) and the CI no-egress check named in T-7/SR-5.

findings.finding: Chunking is listed test-first, but the two pinned-with-fallback chunkers (CodeHierarchyNodeParser pinned + tree-sitter fallback, D-24) have no equivalence/fallback-trigger test named, and the dual-register ('plain'|'deep') chunk variants (DATA_MODEL register field) have no test asserting both registers are produced/queryable. The 'context augmentation' branch (voyage-context-3 cloud vs late-chunking local) is a provider fork that changes chunk content but has no determinism/parity test seam.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§8,§17; DECISIONS.md:D-24; DATA_MODEL.md:18 (register dual-register); RESEARCH.md:9
findings.proposed_fix: Name a chunker test: CodeHierarchyNodeParser primary vs tree-sitter fallback produce structurally compatible anchored chunks on a fixture repo (and the fallback-trigger condition is asserted), and both 'plain'/'deep' registers are emitted. Treat the context-augmentation provider fork like the embedding fork — fixed-input parity test via FakeContextStrategy.

findings.finding: EVALUATION_CRITERIA says the harness 'runs via the cc-crew /eval path' and scores live in Langfuse (local), while D-22/CONSTRAINTS C-10 say the eval harness + Langfuse are dev/CI-only and NOT shipped. It is unclear whether the CI environment is expected to stand up a local Langfuse + OTel Collector to run the gated evals, and whether the merge-blocking redaction-fuzz / unit gates can run WITHOUT that backend (they should, being pure code). If the trust-eval release gate depends on a Langfuse instance in CI, that is an unstated CI infra dependency and a potential flakiness/secrets surface; if not, the scoring/aggregation path for the gate is unspecified.
findings.evidence: EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md:8-9; DECISIONS.md:D-10,D-22; CONSTRAINTS.md:C-10; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§14,§17
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm with the owner: (a) does the CI release gate require a live Langfuse/Collector, or do custom evaluators compute pass/fail in-process and merely export to Langfuse when present? (b) which gates are backend-free (merge: unit + redaction fuzz) vs backend-dependent (release: trust/retrieval evals)? Document the CI infra dependency (or its absence) explicitly so the eval gate is reproducible offline.

summary: The draft names every packaging surface (signed/notarized .dmg/.app, Homebrew Cask, bundled PyInstaller sidecar, CLI via pipx/brew, Tauri auto-updater) and treats install/setup/uninstall reasonably (Flow 1/12, FR-19..FR-22, T-8). But for the PRODUCTION-GRADE no-timebox posture, deploy/rollback is materially UNDERSPECIFIED in the hardest, most dangerous places. Most load-bearing gap: there is NO contract for auto-updating the app WHILE the sidecar is running and an in-flight re-index/upsert is mid-batch — §16 says 'auto-updater' and nothing else — which collides head-on with the D-25 single-writer + optimize()-after-batch contract and the blue-green generation invariant. Second: NO schema-migration / backup / rollback contract for the local stores — manifest.schemaVersion and LanceDB store-level schema_version exist, but no doc says how a NEW binary migrates an OLD on-disk manifest/registry/LanceDB dataset forward, or rolls back on downgrade/migration failure. Third: the LanceDB Lance-format / lancedb-pin UPGRADE path has a steady-state maintenance contract but NO procedure for when the pinned version itself moves (Lance format 2.1 bump, lancedb pre-1.0 alpha churn). Fourth: the notarization gate 'follows the NexusOps sidecar precedent' — but NexusOps's own ARCHITECTURE marks that precedent UNSOLVED ([LOCKED-PENDING-SPIKE], issue #11992); the precedent does not yet exist. Fifth: update-channel integrity (appcast/Sparkle key, Cask sha256 auto-bump), staged-rollout/bad-release rollback, and Tauri-shell↔sidecar version-skew are all unspecified.

findings.finding: No contract for Tauri auto-update WHILE the Python sidecar is running with an in-flight re-index/upsert. §16 lists '(auto-updater)' with zero behavior. The desktop app is a two-process system (Tauri shell + daemon-owned PyInstaller sidecar, D-17). A Tauri/Sparkle update replaces the .app and relaunches; if the sidecar is mid-batch it is in the exact window the D-25 maintenance contract calls dangerous: a LanceDB upsert batch not yet folded by optimize() leaves num_unindexed_rows>0 (W1: fast_search silently excludes them), or a blue-green re-embed is mid-swap. Killing the sidecar there can leave a half-written generation, an un-optimized index that silently drops rows, or an optimistic-commit conflict (W3). No quiesce/drain/resume handshake, no 'refuse-update-until-writers-idle' gate, no post-update integrity check is specified.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §16 ('(auto-updater)'); DECISIONS.md D-25 (optimize()-after-batch, num_unindexed_rows, single-writer, blue-green); DATA_MODEL.md 'Index generation' state machine; DECISIONS.md D-17 (daemon-owned sidecar lifecycle)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a §16 update-lifecycle contract: on update, the Tauri shell signals the sidecar to (a) stop accepting new ingest, (b) drain/commit + optimize() the current batch or atomically abandon to the prior generation, (c) checkpoint the content-hash resume manifest, (d) ack 'safe-to-replace'; only then swap the .app and relaunch; on relaunch run a store-integrity check (num_unindexed_rows fold, generation-pointer validity) before serving. Define a max-drain timeout and the fallback (force-quit → idempotent resume from the content-hash manifest per Flow 9/10).

findings.finding: No on-disk schema MIGRATION + backup/rollback contract for the local stores. manifest.json carries schemaVersion and the LanceDB store stamp carries schema_version, and federation 'gates on schema_version → refuse/flag mismatch' — but that gate only EXCLUDES a mismatched store; it does not migrate it. There is no spec for how a newly-installed app binary upgrades an OLDER on-disk manifest/registry/LanceDB dataset to the new schema, no backup-before-migrate step, and no rollback if migration fails or the user downgrades (e.g. reverts the Cask). NexusOps solved exactly this for its store via SQLite `user_version` migrations (NexusOps ARCH decision 003); the Brain draft has no analogue for any of its three stores. The reproducibility invariant ('rebuild from source') is the only stated escape hatch, but a full portfolio rebuild on every schema bump is not an acceptable production migration story.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md ① store stamp {schema_version}, ③ manifest {schemaVersion}, global registry {schema_version}; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §9 (gate on schema_version → refuse/flag); OPEN_QUESTIONS.md OQ-6 ('freeze manifest schema at first ingest slice'); NexusOps ARCHITECTURE.md decision 003 (user_version migrations) — the named precedent the Brain lacks
findings.proposed_fix: Add a migrations contract to §7/§16: a forward-only migration runner keyed on schemaVersion for manifest + registry, and a documented procedure for LanceDB store-stamp schema bumps (in-place metadata migration vs blue-green rebuild, per D-14). Require backup-before-migrate (copy or LanceDB version tag) and define rollback: on migration failure, restore the pre-migration version tag / backup and surface a clear error; on app downgrade, refuse-with-guidance rather than silently reading a forward-schema store. Make OQ-6 a blocking decision, not a 'freeze later'.

findings.finding: The LanceDB format / lancedb-pin UPGRADE path is unspecified. D-25/C-6 correctly PIN lancedb (pre-1.0 alpha on Lance format 2.1) and the maintenance contract covers steady state, but there is no procedure for when the pin itself moves: a lancedb minor that flips the handle pattern (TR-3 names this), or a Lance file-format bump beyond 2.1. The blue-green re-embed contract (D-14) covers embedding-model/dim changes, NOT a storage-format/library upgrade — those are different triggers needing a different (read-old-format → rewrite-new-format) migration. Without this, bumping the pin on a release risks every existing user's datasets failing to open after auto-update.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md D-25 (pin lancedb; Lance format 2.1; 'data safe on format 2.1'); CONSTRAINTS.md C-6; RISKS.md TR-3 (handle pattern flipped across minors); ASSUMPTIONS.md A-1; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §7 (blue-green only on model/dim change)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a 'storage-format/library upgrade' procedure distinct from blue-green re-embed: on a lancedb/Lance-format pin bump, the release must include a compatibility-checked migration (open-old → verify → optionally rewrite) gated by a CI test that opens a fixture dataset written by the PREVIOUS pinned version. Record the lance format version in the store stamp so the runtime can detect and refuse/auto-migrate. Treat a Lance major-format bump as a blocking pre-release spike.

findings.finding: The notarization gate rests on a precedent that does not yet exist. §16 and TR-8/A-7 say to 'follow the NexusOps sidecar precedent' for notarizing the PyInstaller core under hardened runtime — but NexusOps's own ARCHITECTURE marks macOS Python-sidecar notarization as an UNSOLVED, pre-build spike: decision 005 is `[LOCKED-PENDING-SPIKE]` (issue #11992, OQ-PLAT-SPIKE-1), explicitly listed among 'the hardest risks ... not silently assumed away.' So Brain is leaning on a precedent that is itself still a managed risk. This is a shared ship-blocker, not a settled pattern, and the draft presents it as settled.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §16 ('follow the NexusOps sidecar precedent [PH]'); RISKS.md TR-8; ASSUMPTIONS.md A-7 ('[ASSUMPTION] NexusOps does this ... precedent'); NexusOps ARCHITECTURE.md decision 005 [LOCKED-PENDING-SPIKE] #11992 + §24 spike list
findings.proposed_fix: Reclassify A-7/TR-8 from 'assumption/precedent' to a Brain-side pre-build SPIKE (deep-sign order for the embedded Python interpreter + dylibs, hardened-runtime entitlements for PyInstaller, stapling, spctl gate). Either co-own NexusOps issue #11992 or run an independent Brain notarization spike before committing the bundle topology; do not treat NexusOps as having solved it.

findings.finding: Update-channel integrity and bad-release rollback are entirely unspecified. There is an auto-updater and a Homebrew Cask, but no appcast/update-feed signing story (Sparkle EdDSA key custody, or Tauri-updater pubkey + where the signing key lives), no Cask sha256/version auto-bump-and-verify procedure, no staged rollout, and no mechanism to roll BACK a bad release once auto-update has pushed it to users. A privacy-first local tool with auto-update is a supply-chain target (T-12 names dep supply-chain but not the UPDATE channel itself). Without channel integrity, the auto-updater is itself an attack/foot-gun surface.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §16 (auto-updater + Homebrew Cask, no integrity detail); THREAT_MODEL.md T-12 (supply-chain — deps only, not the update channel); DECISIONS.md D-20 ('with the auto-updater'); D-22/C-13 (no phone-home — constrains telemetry but says nothing about the update feed)
findings.proposed_fix: Specify: (1) signed update feed (Tauri updater pubkey or Sparkle EdDSA), key custody in CI secrets, signature verified before apply; (2) Cask version+sha256 auto-bump gated on the notarized artifact hash; (3) a bad-release rollback path (yank the appcast entry / pin Cask to last-good + user-facing 'downgrade' that respects the D10-2 downgrade-refusal rule); (4) optionally staged rollout. Add an update-channel-integrity row to THREAT_MODEL.md.

findings.finding: No Tauri-shell ↔ bundled-sidecar version-skew contract. The app bundles a specific PyInstaller core, but the CLI is ALSO installable independently via pipx/brew formula (D-20), and the loopback/stdio protocol (D-5) crosses the shell↔core boundary. Nothing pins the shell to a compatible core version, nor defines behavior when an independently-updated pipx CLI / headless core is older or newer than the store schema it opens, or when the Tauri shell talks to a sidecar of a mismatched protocol version. Independent update channels (Cask vs pipx vs brew formula) make skew a normal occurrence, not an edge case.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md D-20 (CLI installable via bundle AND pipx AND brew formula); D-17 (two-process shell↔sidecar over loopback/stdio); D-21 (published nexus-brain-core as a versioned dep)
findings.proposed_fix: Define a version-compatibility handshake at the loopback/stdio boundary (shell announces required core API/protocol range; core announces its version; mismatch → refuse with guidance). Document the supported-skew policy across the three install channels and what happens when a separately-installed CLI/core is older/newer than the store schema (ties to D10-2's downgrade-refusal).

findings.finding: Uninstall/reset (Flow 12, FR-22, T-8) reverses HOST-config mutations (MCP registration, skills, keychain refs) but does not address deploy-layer reversal: removing the PATH symlink created at setup (D-20 'PATH-symlinked at setup'), removing the auto-update's installed update artifacts/caches, the launchd agent if the sidecar is registered as an OS service (D-16 names launchd∥systemd as the service seam), or git-hooks installed per-repo at `add` (Flow 2 step 7 / R-ADD step 5). 'Reverse every host mutation' is asserted but the enumerated reversal list is incomplete for a production uninstaller.
findings.evidence: USER_FLOWS.md Flow 12; REQUIREMENTS.md FR-22; THREAT_MODEL.md T-8; DECISIONS.md D-20 (PATH symlink), D-16 (launchd service seam); USER_FLOWS.md Flow 2 step 7 + ONBOARDING R-ADD step 5 (per-repo git hooks)
findings.proposed_fix: Expand Flow 12 to enumerate and reverse: PATH symlink, launchd/systemd service registration, per-repo git hooks installed at `add`, update-cache/artifacts, and the MCP/skills/keychain entries already listed. Track every install-time mutation in a manifest (Flow 1 already 'tracks host config entries for reversal' — extend that ledger to ALL of the above) so uninstall is the literal inverse.

findings.finding: No backup story for the registry/manifest as a single point of failure across upgrades. The global registry (~/.project-brain/registry) is what the federation router reads to fan out; the per-repo manifest is the catalog of hashes/versions. LanceDB datasets have native versioning/restore (D-14) and source is reproducible, but a corrupted or partially-migrated registry/manifest (e.g. crash during the D10-2 migration, or a bad auto-update) has no backup/restore path specified. The reproducibility invariant covers the LanceDB index, not the registry/manifest catalog state (project→db_path mappings, policy paths, last_indexed_sha).
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md ③ ('~/.project-brain/ registry: project_id → {db_path, schema_version, model_version, codegraph_db_path, last_indexed_sha, policy}' — 'what the federation router reads'); reproducibility invariants (cover the index, not the catalog)
findings.proposed_fix: Specify backup-before-mutate for registry/manifest writes (write-temp-then-atomic-rename + keep last-good copy), and a rebuild-registry-from-manifests recovery path (scan ~/.project-brain/ for per-project manifests → reconstruct the registry) so a corrupt registry is recoverable without re-adding every project. Make this part of the §18 failure-modes table.

findings.finding: The Linux deploy path (AppImage/tarball + systemd --user, D-16/D-20) inherits ALL of the above gaps with zero Linux-specific deploy/update/rollback detail. It is correctly deferred, but the draft should explicitly mark that the auto-update, notarization-equivalent (no macOS notarization on Linux; instead checksum/GPG-sig of the AppImage), and schema-migration contracts must be re-derived for the systemd path rather than assumed-equivalent.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md D-16, D-20 (Linux later: AppImage/tarball + systemd --user); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §16 ('Linux later')
findings.proposed_fix: Add a one-line note in §16 that the Linux follow-on must re-specify update-feed integrity (AppImage GPG/zsync), service-lifecycle quiesce for systemd --user (the D10-1 drain contract), and uninstall reversal for the systemd unit — these are NOT macOS-equivalent and are not free.

findings.finding: CI release gates (§17) omit deploy-specific gates the production posture implies: an on-disk migration+rollback test (open an OLD-version store with the NEW binary), an arm64-wheel/spawn-not-fork smoke on the ACTUAL notarized bundle (D-25 names arm64 wheel verification only as a maintenance-contract item, not a release gate), a Cask install/uninstall reversal smoke, and an auto-update E2E. Without these the pipeline can ship a binary that builds and evals green yet corrupts an existing user's store on first launch.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §17 (CI gates: unit/redaction/eval/notarization); DECISIONS.md D-25 (arm64 wheels in CI; spawn not fork); REQUIREMENTS.md PH-1
findings.proposed_fix: Add release-gate rows to §17: (1) old-store→new-binary migration+rollback test; (2) notarized-bundle launch smoke (sidecar spawns under hardened runtime, arm64, spawn mode); (3) Cask install→uninstall reversal smoke; (4) auto-update E2E (signed feed → update → in-flight-write quiesce → resume). Tie to D10-1/D10-2/D10-3/D10-4/D10-5.

findings.finding: Who owns the macOS Apple Developer ID / notarization signing identity and the update-feed signing key, and is it shared with NexusOps or Brain-specific? NexusOps treats Developer ID signing + notarization as an 'early release-blocker' (decision 011) and Brain is positioned to 'follow the precedent', but the docs never state whether the two products share one signing identity/team, one appcast key, and one CI secrets store, or each maintain their own. This is load-bearing for the release pipeline, the supply-chain trust story, and the eventual integrated-bundle question.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §16; DECISIONS.md D-20; NexusOps ARCHITECTURE.md decision 011 (Developer ID signing + notarization = early release-blocker); no signing-identity ownership statement in any Brain planning doc
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm with the owner: shared vs separate Apple Developer ID, notarization profile, and update-feed signing key between Nexus Brain and NexusOps; where the keys live in CI; and whether the eventual integrated product re-uses Brain's notarized sidecar or re-notarizes under NexusOps's identity.

summary: The draft's security spine is coherent and aligns with NexusOps's three laws, but the loopback-HTTP transport (D-5) is asserted without a threat model: no replay protection, token lifetime/scope/at-rest-permissions, transport peer-auth, or browser-specific Origin/DNS-rebinding defenses are specified — and the draft offers no stdio peer-auth analog to NexusOps's mandatory getpeereid() check. The single most load-bearing inconsistency: THREAT_MODEL T-1/SR-1 promise a "zero leaks (hard gate)" redaction outcome and claim to "mirror NexusOps's prefix-entropy-v3" — but the actual NexusOps §15 engine it inherits has a MEASURED recall envelope of recall_catchable=1.0 / overall=0.75 with three named, accepted-and-owned residual leak classes. The redaction promise must be reframed to "zero leaks on the catchable set" + keychain-refs-as-primary-control, or it is an undeliverable gate. Bounded-allowlist enforcement is named as an invariant but no chokepoint/authorization code path is specified (unlike NexusOps's Gateway). Supply-chain has version-pinning but no provenance/checksum/signature verification for setup-pulled models or CodeGraph. The integrated path is consistent with INV-SEC-1.

findings.finding: THREAT_MODEL claims a 'zero leaks (hard gate)' redaction outcome (T-1, SR-1) and says it 'Mirrors NexusOps's prefix-entropy-v3 approach so the integrated path inherits a proven engine' — but the actual NexusOps prefix-entropy-v3 engine it inherits has a MEASURED recall envelope of recall_catchable 1.0 / overall 0.75, with three accepted-and-owned residual leak classes (hex≈git-SHA, adversarial split-into-<20-char, sub-20-char JSON values). A 'zero leaks' hard CI gate over arbitrary inputs is therefore undeliverable by the very engine cited; the honest contract is 'zero leaks on the catchable set, keychain-refs-only as the primary control, redactor as defense-in-depth.' As written, either the eval gate blocks every release (it can never hit zero on the full residual surface) or it is quietly scoped to 'catchable' without saying so — a trust hole.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md:T-1 + Redaction engine (MVP) section ('zero leaks (hard gate)', 'Mirrors NexusOps's prefix-entropy-v3'); RISKS.md:SR-1 ('Redaction-recall fuzz = zero leaks (hard gate)'); contradicted by NexusOps/ARCHITECTURE.md §15 Redaction-before-persist ('recall_catchable 1.0 ... overall 0.75 ... surviving residuals (b)(c)(<20ch-a) ... stay accepted; primary control remains keychain-refs-only')
findings.proposed_fix: Reframe the redaction gate in THREAT_MODEL T-1, the Redaction-engine section, RISKS SR-1, and EVALUATION_CRITERIA to: (a) 'zero leaks on the catchable set' as the hard gate (recall-floor on the curated prefix/entropy/JSON-value classes), (b) explicitly enumerate the accepted residual classes inherited from prefix-entropy-v3 (≈git-SHA hex, adversarial <20-char split, sub-20-char JSON values) as ACCEPTED-AND-OWNED, and (c) name keychain-refs-only as the load-bearing primary control with the redactor as defense-in-depth. State the living-regression-fixture recall-floor/FP-ceiling as the CI assertion, not literal zero over arbitrary input.

findings.finding: The loopback HTTP transport (D-5: 127.0.0.1 + per-launch token) is asserted as the control against 'other local users/processes' but has NO threat model. Unspecified: (1) replay protection — a per-launch token sent as a bearer over a loopback HTTP request is replayable by any local process that can read it or observe it; nothing says the token is single-use, time-bound, or bound to a connection/nonce; (2) token-at-rest protection — where the per-launch token lives (env var readable via /proc-equivalent or `ps`? a file? what perms?) is undefined, so 'other local users/processes' (the named adversary) can potentially read it; (3) Origin/DNS-rebinding defense — D-17 explicitly allows a browser/WebView UI over this transport, so a malicious web page in any browser can issue cross-origin requests to 127.0.0.1 and (absent Origin allowlisting / a non-simple custom header requirement) exfiltrate raw-code retrieval results. The trust boundary T-6 leans entirely on this token, so its silent under-specification is load-bearing.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md:T-6 ('loopback HTTP gated by a per-launch token. (D-5/T-3)') and 'Out of scope: ... remote/network attackers beyond localhost' (does NOT exclude other LOCAL processes/users, which §Trust-boundary lists as in-scope); DECISIONS.md:D-5; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§4.1, §11; no replay/lifetime/Origin/at-rest spec anywhere (grep across ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT/THREAT_MODEL/DECISIONS/USER_FLOWS/DOMAIN_MODEL found only the bare 'per-launch token' phrase)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a THREAT_MODEL sub-section specifying the loopback-HTTP token contract: (a) token = high-entropy, per-launch, stored only in memory or a 0600 file the sidecar+Tauri share (never an env var visible to `ps`/other-uid); (b) bound to the connection/handshake and rejected on reuse beyond its session, with a documented lifetime; (c) for the browser/WebView case, mandate an Origin/Host allowlist AND require a non-simple custom auth header (so cross-origin simple requests are blocked by CORS preflight + the page can't forge it) — i.e. an explicit DNS-rebinding defense; (d) state the local-process threat: any same-uid process is inside the trust boundary by D-5 (single OS user), so document that 'other local users' (distinct uid) is the actual boundary and that the token defends a same-machine-different-uid or browser-page attacker, not a same-uid process. Confirm whether same-uid-other-process is in-scope or accepted.

findings.finding: No stdio/loopback peer-auth analog to NexusOps's mandatory getpeereid() is specified. NexusOps INV/§15 requires UDS peer-auth via getpeereid() rejecting uid != daemon-uid as the PRIMARY control (socket perms are only defense-in-depth). The Brain's stdio MCP transport inherits parent-process trust implicitly (whoever spawned the sidecar), but the draft never states this, and the opt-in loopback transport has no peer-identity check at all beyond the token. For the integrated path, NexusOps owns the sidecar lifecycle and peer-auth — but for the STANDALONE path, the question 'does the standalone MCP server authenticate its caller, or trust any local connector?' is unanswered. T-6 says redaction+policy happens 'regardless of caller,' which is the right defense-in-depth, but caller-identity is still undefined for audit/policy attribution.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md:T-6 (redact 'regardless of caller' — but no caller authentication); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§11 (stdio default, opt loopback+token); contrast NexusOps/ARCHITECTURE.md §15 ('UDS peer-auth (macOS): getpeereid() ... reject uid != daemon-uid; socket perms ... are defense-in-depth, not the primary control')
findings.proposed_fix: State the standalone MCP peer-trust model explicitly in THREAT_MODEL: stdio = parent-process-trust (the spawning process IS the authenticated caller; redaction+policy at the boundary is the egress control); loopback = the token IS the caller credential. Decide and record whether the standalone MCP server needs uid-level peer-auth on the loopback path (recommended: yes — reject connections whose peer uid differs from the sidecar uid, mirroring getpeereid(), since the loopback socket is otherwise reachable by any local uid). Note that integrated-mode peer-auth is NexusOps's responsibility (daemon-owned lifecycle).

findings.finding: The bounded-mutation allowlist is asserted as a binding invariant (T-5, architectural law #3, D-4) but its ENFORCEMENT mechanism is never specified. NexusOps enforces its equivalent through a single named chokepoint (the Action Gateway: every mutation is a typed Action through one pipeline, tested by a §14 architecture-invariant test that 'no executor is reachable except via the Gateway pipeline'). The Brain draft names HostPort as 'authorizes + performs side-effects' (§12, D-21) but never states HOW the allowlist is enforced as a chokepoint: is there a single mutation path through StandaloneHost that all writes funnel through? Is there an architecture-invariant test proving no module raw-mutates the filesystem/git outside HostPort? Architectural law #3 says 'the core never raw-mutates' — but nothing names the mechanism or the test that makes 'never' true. SR-4's signal is 'Unauthorized-mutation = 0' with no described enforcement to produce that zero.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md:T-5; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§4.2 law 3 ('Side-effects flow only through the active HostPort ... the core never raw-mutates') and §12 (HostPort 'authorizes + performs side-effects'); RISKS.md:SR-4; DECISIONS.md:D-4 — none specify a single-chokepoint enforcement or invariant test; contrast NexusOps/ARCHITECTURE.md §15 INV-SEC-1 ('no executor reachable except via the Gateway pipeline ... Tested by the §14 architecture-invariant test')
findings.proposed_fix: Specify the StandaloneHost enforcement chokepoint explicitly: all FS/git/host-config side-effects MUST route through a single HostPort.perform(action) method taking a typed, allowlist-checked mutation enum; the core has no direct fs/git-write imports. Add an architecture-invariant test (mirroring NexusOps §14) asserting no core module reaches a mutation primitive except via HostPort. Define the allowlist as a closed typed enum (own-store-write | owned-doc-refresh | consented-host-config) so 'everything else' fails closed by type. Name this in §12/§15 and as the SR-4 signal's actual mechanism.

findings.finding: Supply-chain (T-12, C-5..C-11) covers version-PINNING and license-gating but has NO provenance/integrity verification for the largest attack surface: the multi-GB models and external binaries that `setup` pulls at install time. FR-20 / D-20 say setup 'detect→install→verify' for CodeGraph + Ollama + model, but 'verify' is undefined — there is no checksum/digest pin, no signature check, no model-provenance assertion. An attacker who can MITM or poison an Ollama model pull, a CodeGraph download channel (A-5 notes the channel is itself unconfirmed — 'may NOT be an npm package'), or a Homebrew tap delivers code that runs inside the trust boundary with full repo + keychain-adjacent access. This is the classic production-grade supply-chain baseline the posture says is non-deferrable.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md:T-12 ('Pin versions; verify on install; provision via real channels'); REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-20 ('detect→install→verify'); DECISIONS.md:D-20 ('detect→install→verify, idempotent + consented'); ASSUMPTIONS.md:A-5 (CodeGraph channel unconfirmed); CONSTRAINTS.md:C-5..C-11 — none specify a digest/signature/SBOM check for pulled models or the CodeGraph binary
findings.proposed_fix: Define 'verify' in T-12/D-20/FR-20 as a concrete integrity contract: pin model + dep artifacts by SHA-256 digest (or the upstream's signed manifest) and fail setup closed on mismatch; for Ollama models, record and verify the model blob digest in the store-level version stamp; for CodeGraph, pin the release artifact hash once the channel is resolved (A-5); prefer signature verification where the channel supports it; record a per-install provenance manifest (artifact, source URL, digest, verified-at). Treat an unverifiable pull as a hard setup failure, not a warning.

findings.finding: The MCP boundary's redaction guarantee may have a hole at `get_file` / whole-file hydration. T-6 says the MCP server redacts at the boundary 'regardless of caller,' and D-15 says the same redactor gates MCP-boundary egress. But the retrieval pipeline (§8, D-13) does WHOLE-FILE HYDRATION and the MCP server exposes a `get_file` tool (§11) that returns file contents. Redaction-before-EMBED operates on chunks at index time; whole-file hydration reads RAW files from disk at query time (the index is a cache, not source-of-truth — law 1). It is unstated whether raw hydrated file content and `get_file` responses pass through the redactor before leaving the MCP boundary, or only the embedded/indexed chunks are redacted. If a secret survived in a file (e.g. one of the accepted residual classes, or a file excluded from indexing) it can egress raw via get_file/hydration even though the index was clean.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§8 ('whole-file hydration'), §11 (MCP exposes 'get_file'), §4.2 law 1 ('index is a cache ... reproducible from source'); THREAT_MODEL.md:T-6/D-15 (redaction 'before embed' + 'gates the MCP-boundary egress' — but whole-file hydration reads source, not the index)
findings.proposed_fix: State explicitly in §8/§11/T-6 that ALL MCP egress paths — including raw whole-file hydration and get_file responses — pass through the same Redactor before results leave the boundary, not only index-time chunk redaction. Add a redaction-recall eval case over the get_file/hydration path (raw source → boundary), distinct from the embed-path fuzz corpus. If hydration deliberately returns raw (for fidelity), make that an explicit, documented residual with the keychain-refs-only primary control called out.

findings.finding: The integrated-mode security story is consistent with NexusOps INV-SEC-1 (propose-only, Gateway-routed, redacted-outbox-consumed, never sets ExecutionProfile) and contradicts nothing in the ground-truth platform docs — confirmed against NexusOps §13.1, §4.2, §15 and PROJECT_BRAIN_INTERFACE §8. One minor residual: the THREAT_MODEL integrated-mode deltas say inputs 'arrive already redacted' but the standalone Redactor and the NexusOps Redactor are two independently-evolving engines (Python prefix-entropy-v3 reimplementation vs Rust prefix-entropy-v3); the claim 'the SAME redactor / same engine' is aspirational, not literal — a version-skew between the two engines could mean the standalone path and integrated path have different residual surfaces. Worth flagging that engine-parity must be tested at the seam, not assumed.
