findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md:Integrated-mode deltas ('the standalone Redactor is the same engine'); MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:§8 ('When integrated, inputs arrive already redacted ... Standalone, Project Brain runs its own redactor'); NexusOps/ARCHITECTURE.md §15 (Rust prefix-entropy-v3) vs Brain redactor (Python) — two implementations
findings.proposed_fix: Soften 'the same engine' to 'the same redaction CONTRACT (recall-floor/FP-ceiling + residual classes), enforced by parity tests against a shared fuzz corpus.' Add a cross-engine parity test to the seam contract (IR-1) so a divergence between the Python standalone redactor and the Rust NexusOps redactor is caught, since the integrated path trusts NexusOps's redaction and the standalone path trusts its own.

findings.finding: The trust boundary section names 'other local users/processes' implicitly (Trust boundary = 'single OS user'; out-of-scope = 'remote/network attackers beyond localhost' — which does NOT exclude same-machine different-uid users or same-uid different processes). It is ambiguous whether a same-uid malicious process (e.g. a compromised dependency in another tool the user runs) is in-scope. D-5 ('single OS user') implies same-uid = trusted, but T-6's whole premise ('untrusted MCP caller') implies some local callers are untrusted. These two framings are in tension and determine whether the loopback token and MCP peer-auth (D11-2/D11-3) need to defend against same-uid processes at all.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md:Trust boundary ('single OS user (D-5)') + Out-of-scope ('remote/network attackers beyond localhost') vs T-6 ('Untrusted MCP caller'); DECISIONS.md:D-5 ('One OS user, one machine')
findings.proposed_fix: Owner to confirm the local adversary scope: (A) same-uid is fully trusted (then the loopback token only defends different-uid local users + browser pages, and same-uid-process exfil is an accepted non-goal — state it), or (B) same-uid untrusted processes are in-scope (then the token must be unreadable by other same-uid processes, which is hard on macOS — likely requires a different mechanism). Record the chosen posture so D11-2/D11-3 can be specified against a definite threat.

dimension: D12 — Missing diagrams the design needs (DIAGRAM_PLAN vs subsystems/flows)
summary: DIAGRAM_PLAN's D1–D9 cover the steady-state spine well (topology, ports/seam, three-store data plane, retrieval+grounding, federation, sync/drift, observability, security boundary, state machines). But four production-grade subsystems/flows that the architecture treats as first-class (per C-17: install/lifecycle, deploy/rollback, propose-only seam) have NO dedicated planned diagram, and one named flow is only partially covered. Most load-bearing gaps: (1) the install/bootstrap/uninstall sequence (Flow 1/12, FR-19..22) — a §16 first-class subsystem with a NexusOps sidecar precedent, no diagram; (2) the NexusOps ActionPlan propose-only protocol (Flow 11, §20, MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE §7) — only a dashed edge in D1/D8, no sequence; (3) the session-ingestion/episode-card pipeline (Flow 7, FR-14) — D4's index lane is code/docs only; (4) the blue-green re-embed / provider-switch / version-stamp flow (Flow 9, PH-5) — the prompt's named example, only partially covered by D9's Index-generation state machine. The desktop two-process sidecar-lifecycle is a borderline nice-to-have. None of these change a contract by themselves (the underlying decisions exist); they are diagram-coverage gaps that the finalize/build pass must fill so the operability/seam stories are auditable.

findings.finding: No diagram for the install/bootstrap/uninstall lifecycle. Flow 1 (setup) and Flow 12 (uninstall) — detect→install→verify CodeGraph; provision Ollama+model; register MCP into ~/.claude.json and ~/.codex/config.toml; register skills; create ~/.project-brain registry; store keys in keychain — plus the symmetric reversal of every host mutation, are a first-class production-grade subsystem (§16, FR-19/20/21/22, C-17 names deploy/rollback as baseline) with an explicit NexusOps sidecar precedent. DIAGRAM_PLAN has no diagram for it. D8 (security boundary) only shows the keychain as a static node; D1 shows the runtime topology, not the install sequence. The idempotency/reversal/consent property (each mutation tracked so uninstall reverses it — T-8) is exactly what a sequence/flow diagram makes verifiable.
findings.evidence: DIAGRAM_PLAN.md:D1-D9 (no install/lifecycle diagram); USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 1 / Flow 12; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§16; REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-19..FR-22; THREAT_MODEL.md:T-8
findings.proposed_fix: Add a planned diagram 'D10 — Install/bootstrap & uninstall sequence' (type: sequence/flow; illustrates §16 + Flow 1/12): actor→setup→[detect/install/verify CodeGraph]→[provision Ollama+model]→[register MCP+skills, idempotent/consented]→[keychain]→[registry], with the reverse-arrows for uninstall and a 'mutation ledger' note showing every host change is tracked for reversal.

findings.finding: No sequence diagram for the NexusOps ActionPlan / propose-only protocol — the actual cross-product seam protocol. Flow 11 / §20 / MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE §7 specify a concrete multi-step exchange: Brain retrieves context → submit_action_plan (requester_type=project_brain) wrapping catalog ActionRequests → PlanAck → per-step preview + RiskLevel(0–4) → user approves (all/step) → platform executes + emits events → Brain indexes resulting evidence; plus the redacted-outbox consumption. DIAGRAM_PLAN only represents this as a single dashed 'propose-only' edge in D1 and a label in D8 (§15). The prompt explicitly flags the blue-green/seam flows as diagram candidates. A propose-only no-bypass invariant (INV-SEC-1) that is the whole point of the integration is worth a protocol/sequence diagram so the no-execute boundary is auditable.
findings.evidence: DIAGRAM_PLAN.md:D1 (dashed propose-only edge only), D8; USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 11; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§12/§20; MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:§7; REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-18 [P2]
findings.proposed_fix: Add 'D11 — Propose-only ActionPlan seam (sequence)' (illustrates §20 + Flow 11 + MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE §7): Brain→submit_action_plan→Gateway PlanAck→preview+risk(0–4)→user approve→execute→events→outbox→Brain-indexes, marked [P2]. Even as [P2], drawing it now de-risks the day-one HostPort seam (D-21 'build the seam from day one').

findings.finding: No diagram for the session-ingestion / episode-card pipeline. D4 (retrieval & grounding pipeline) explicitly draws only the code/docs index lane (discover→classify→chunk→context-aug→redact→embed→LanceDB→optimize). Flow 7 / FR-14 / D-15 define a distinct, security-sensitive lane — consent-gate (stricter than docs/code) → read transcript → redact + EXCLUDE thinking blocks (raw transcripts never embedded) → summarize → episode card (Brain-owned) → embed (local default) → commit-link with confidence, with a quarantine path on high-confidence-unsafe spans. This lane has its own trust controls (T-4) and is absent from every planned diagram; D9's state-machine set lists Index/Anchor/Project/Worker/Doc lifecycle but no episode/session machine.
findings.evidence: DIAGRAM_PLAN.md:D4 (index lane = code/docs only), D9 (no session/episode state machine); USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 7; REQUIREMENTS.md:FR-14; THREAT_MODEL.md:T-4; DATA_MODEL.md:Episode Card
findings.proposed_fix: Add 'D12 — Session ingestion → episode-card pipeline' (flow; illustrates Flow 7 + §11 sessions module): consent-gate→read→redact(+exclude thinking)→quarantine-branch→summarize→episode card→embed→commit-link(confidence). Optionally annotate the 'raw transcripts never embedded' and consent-gate boundaries to tie it to D8.

findings.finding: The provider-switch / version-stamp / blue-green re-embed flow — explicitly named in the prompt as a likely gap — is only partially diagrammed. D9 plans an 'Index generation' state machine and D3 shows 'version tags / blue-green generations' as data-plane nodes, but no diagram captures the operational FLOW of Flow 9 / PH-5 / NFR-2: detect via store stamp → branch (dim-mismatch → hard rebuild vs same-dim/new-model → re-embed) → write NEW versioned generation → atomic swap of the query path → keep old for rollback (restore(version)) → idempotent resume of only missing content-hash deltas → on crash, prior generation stays active. This is a load-bearing correctness/crash-safety path (T-11, PH-4) and the reproducibility invariant (NFR-2 'no in-place re-embed').
findings.evidence: DIAGRAM_PLAN.md:D3 (nodes only), D9 (state machine, not the operational flow); USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 9; REQUIREMENTS.md:PH-4/PH-5/NFR-2; DATA_MODEL.md:Reproducibility invariants 2; THREAT_MODEL.md:T-11
findings.proposed_fix: Either extend D9's Index-generation state machine into an explicit blue-green sequence (detect-stamp→dim-branch→new-generation→atomic-swap→rollback→crash-resume) or add a dedicated 'Blue-green re-embed / provider switch' flow diagram. Make the dim-mismatch-vs-same-dim branch and the atomic-swap/rollback explicit, since these are the tested correctness invariants.

findings.finding: The two-process desktop runtime LIFECYCLE (Tauri shell ↔ Python sidecar) is shown statically in D1 (topology with a loopback-HTTP/stdio edge) but no diagram captures the dynamic sidecar lifecycle: daemon-owned spawn, per-launch loopback-token handshake (D-5), health/readiness, restart-on-crash, and the D-25 W5 'periodically recycle the sidecar' / spawn-not-fork operability requirements. For a production-grade two-process app this is the kind of operability detail a small lifecycle diagram clarifies, but D1 plus the prose may be judged sufficient.
findings.evidence: DIAGRAM_PLAN.md:D1 (static topology); DECISIONS.md:D-17 (two-process), D-25 W5 (recycle the sidecar, spawn-not-fork); CONSTRAINTS.md:C-2
findings.proposed_fix: Optionally add a small 'Desktop sidecar lifecycle' inset (spawn→token-handshake→health→recycle→crash-restart) or annotate D1 with the lifecycle states. Lower priority than D12-1..D12-4.

findings.finding: DIAGRAM_PLAN tags D1–D4 [primary], D5–D8 [secondary], D9 [reference], but states diagrams are 'drawn at finalize/build, not now.' It is unclear whether the four missing diagrams (D12-1..D12-4) are intentionally deferred as build-time artifacts the implementer draws, or genuinely overlooked. If the convention is that only [primary]/[secondary] flows get planned diagrams and lifecycle/seam flows are drawn ad hoc during build, that is a deliberate scope choice the owner should confirm rather than a defect.
findings.evidence: DIAGRAM_PLAN.md:header ('drawn at finalize/build, not now') + D1-D9 tagging; CONSTRAINTS.md:C-17 (install/deploy/rollback are baseline, not deferred)
findings.proposed_fix: Owner to confirm: should the install/uninstall, propose-only seam, session-ingestion, and blue-green flows be added to DIAGRAM_PLAN as planned diagrams now (recommended, given C-17 makes lifecycle/rollback baseline), or are they intentionally left to build-time? If the latter, note that explicitly in the DIAGRAM_PLAN scope line.

summary: The draft's §0–§21 are NOT one-stable-anchor-per-subsystem; they mix thin pointer-sections (§0 ledger, §3 decisions) with overloaded subsystem sections that fuse 2–4 separable subsystems under one anchor — so /tasks-gen cannot cite a precise spec-anchor per phase and the cross-doc-invariants table has no clean §-target. The three structural artifacts the final template mandates for task-planning are ALL absent from the draft: §2.5 subsystem dependency DAG (the source of parallel build tracks), the Spec Anchor Index (REQ-*→§ traceability, despite REQUIREMENTS carrying 36 FR/NFR/PH IDs), and Appendix A model/contract inventory (despite ~10 load-bearing contract models — chunk schema, manifest schema, HostPort + the 6 ports, Anchor, ProvenancePacket, EpisodeCard, ActionPlan wire shape, EvidenceRef — that must be frozen before tracks fork). Several load-bearing areas have NO own anchor at all: install/setup/uninstall lifecycle (Flows 1/12), packaging/distribution, the port-contracts interface surface, sessions/episode cards, and the build-order/sequencing. I recommend an explicit ~24-section §N list below. Severity skews critical/important because /tasks-gen consumes these anchors directly — a missing or coarse anchor produces an untestable, untraceable phase.

findings.finding: The final ARCHITECTURE.md template REQUIRES a §2.5 'Subsystem dependency DAG & parallelization seams' section — it is the SOLE source /tasks-gen uses to derive parallel build tracks (the Track map in IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md reads from it). The draft has NO §2.5 and no import-direction DAG anywhere; §4.1 is a process-topology diagram (deployment), not a subsystem import-DAG, and §6 lists packages without an edge graph. Without §2.5 there is no stable anchor for 'which subsystems may build in parallel' and the team-mode Track map cannot be authored — the whole parallelization plan collapses by default rather than by decision.

findings.proposed_fix: Add §2.5 to the finalized doc: a Mermaid flowchart of the §N subsystem boundaries with import-direction edges (e.g. ports ← ingest/index ← retrieval ← grounding ← agent; federation ← registry; hosts → core). State the import-direction rule (`surfaces → agent → retrieval/grounding → index/ingest → ports/providers/stores`), name the independent subsystems (parallelization seams), and mark which Appendix-A models are crossed by an edge (= shared contracts to freeze before tracks fork). The first vertical slice (D-18) gives the critical path.

Critically anchor the PH rows: PH-1 (maintenance contract)→§Data&LanceDB, PH-2/PH-3→§Security/§Providers, PH-4/PH-5→§Sync&Recovery, PH-6→§Observability, PH-8→§Packaging. Every PH-* must land on exactly one § so it cannot be deferred.

findings.finding: The template's Appendix A (model/contract inventory) is the canonical home for every cross-doc-invariant model and the §2.5 'freeze before tracks fork' set; the area CLAUDE.md cross-doc-invariants table points AT it. The draft has NO Appendix A and no model→§ table, yet there are ~10 load-bearing contract models scattered across DATA_MODEL/DOMAIN_MODEL/PRD with no single anchored owner: the LanceDB chunk schema, the store-level version stamp, .project-brain/manifest.json schema, the global registry schema, Anchor, ProvenancePacket, EpisodeCard, the HostPort + the 6 port interfaces, the ActionPlan internal→wire shape, and the EvidenceRef serialization. At minimum: chunk schema→§Data, version-stamp→§Data, manifest schema→§Data, registry schema→§Federation, Anchor→§Grounding, ProvenancePacket→§Grounding/Retrieval, EpisodeCard→§Sessions, HostPort+6 ports→§Ports/Host-seam, ActionPlan/EvidenceRef→§NexusOps-seam. findings.finding: Draft §8 ('Retrieval & grounding pipeline') fuses THREE separable subsystems under one anchor: index-time ingestion/chunking/embedding, query-time retrieval/rerank/hydration/routing, and the grounding gate / anchor-revalidation / provenance. These have distinct owners, distinct REQs (FR-2..FR-6 ingestion vs FR-7 retrieval vs FR-9/FR-10 grounding), distinct test postures (chunking/redaction are TDD; grounding correctness is eval-gated), and distinct §2.5 dependency positions (ingestion is upstream of retrieval; grounding is downstream of generation). A single §8 anchor forces /tasks-gen to cite the same anchor for unrelated phases and blurs the parallelization seam between the ingest track and the retrieval/grounding track.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §8 (index-time + query-time + grounding gate in one section); REQUIREMENTS FR-2..FR-6 vs FR-7 vs FR-9/FR-10; EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md (chunking/redaction TDD vs grounding eval-gated)
findings.proposed_fix: Split §8 into three anchors: §Ingestion & indexing (discover/classify/chunk/context-aug/redact/embed/LanceDB-write/optimize), §Retrieval & answering (scope/route/hybrid/rerank/graph-tools/hydrate/generate), §Grounding, anchors & provenance (parse/revalidate/gate/provenance-packet — the north star). findings.finding: Draft §11 ('Agent & surfaces') bundles FOUR client surfaces under one anchor: the embedded LlamaIndex agent, the MCP server (the trust boundary), the CLI, and the Tauri desktop UI. These are independent build tracks (the MCP server is a security boundary with its own redaction/policy invariants; the desktop UI is a separate Tauri+ui-kit process; the CLI is headless; the agent is the LlamaIndex Workflow). §19's build order already sequences them as separate steps (4=agent, 5=MCP, 8=desktop UI), so a single §11 anchor cannot be cited per-phase and conflates the MCP trust-boundary contract (FR-13/T-6) with the UI.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §11 (agent+MCP+CLI+UI in one); §19 build order steps 4/5/8 treat them separately; THREAT_MODEL T-6 (MCP boundary) is a distinct invariant; REQUIREMENTS FR-12/FR-13
findings.proposed_fix: Split into: §Embedded agent (LlamaIndex Workflow), §MCP server & trust boundary (the redaction/policy egress — its own anchor because it carries T-6/FR-13 invariants), §CLI & desktop UI surfaces (or two anchors if the Tauri shell warrants its own). The MCP-boundary anchor in particular must stand alone so the cross-doc-invariants table can point a security test at it.

findings.finding: No load-bearing anchor exists for the install/setup/uninstall lifecycle as a SUBSYSTEM. §16 is 'Packaging, install & lifecycle' but it fuses distribution (dmg/brew/PyInstaller) with the setup/add/uninstall host-mutation logic — which is a distinct subsystem carrying binding idempotency/reversibility/consent invariants (T-8, PH-7, FR-19/FR-20/FR-22, Flows 1/12). The host-config mutation surface is a security-relevant subsystem (it writes ~/.claude.json, registers skills, touches the keychain) and deserves its own anchor separate from packaging, so /tasks-gen can cite it and a reversibility test can point at it.

findings.proposed_fix: Split §16 into §Packaging & distribution (signing/notarization/dmg/brew/sidecar bundle) and §Setup, provisioning & lifecycle (setup/add/uninstall; idempotent+reversible+consented host-config; dep provisioning detect→install→verify). Each gets its own anchor.

findings.proposed_fix: Add §Ports & adapter contracts as a first-class anchor owning the 6 port interfaces + the HostPort StandaloneHost/NexusOpsHost split. It becomes the freeze-before-fork contract node in §2.5 and the Appendix-A home for the port signatures.

findings.finding: Sessions / episode cards have no own anchor. The draft mentions sessions only inside §6's package list and §19 step 9; the EpisodeCard is a Brain-OWNED contract model (DATA_MODEL, PRD §7.4, MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE §4) with binding redaction/consent invariants (FR-14, T-4, Flow 7) and a NexusOps serialization. It needs its own §anchor so /tasks-gen can build the opt-in session-ingestion phase against it and the cross-doc table can point the redaction/consent test at it.

findings.finding: Failure modes & recovery (draft §18) and Sync & freshness (§10) are correct as anchors, but §18 is a load-bearing production-grade subsystem (graceful-degradation matrix, atomic-swap crash safety, idempotent resume — PH-4/PH-9, Flows 9/10) that /tasks-gen must be able to cite per-phase; it currently reads as a prose list that defers to RISKS.md. For an anchor to be spec-anchorable it must state the binding behavior inline (the answer-but-flag, retrieval-only-on-model-down, prior-generation-serves-on-crash contracts), not just point elsewhere. Same concern for §0/§3/§5/§7 which are pure pointers — a pointer section is not spec-anchorable.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §18 (defers to RISKS.md), §0/§3/§5/§7 (pointer-only to companion docs); REQUIREMENTS PH-4/PH-9; USER_FLOWS F9/F10
findings.proposed_fix: Keep §Failure modes & recovery and §Sync & freshness as their own anchors but make each STATE the binding degraded-behavior contracts inline (not just a pointer), so a phase can cite the anchor and a test can pin the named behavior. For the pointer sections (§0/§3/§5/§7), keep them but ensure the binding schema/invariant content lives in a spec-anchorable § (Data, Domain) plus Appendix A — the pointer alone cannot be a spec-anchor.

findings.finding: Recommended final §-section list (open-ended §N, one-per-load-bearing-subsystem) to replace the draft's §0–§21 mapping. This is the concrete D13 deliverable. findings.finding: Draft §19 ('MVP boundaries & sequencing') encodes the build order (the first vertical slice D-18 + the 0..10 step sequence) but the final template expects the build order to live in IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md derived FROM the architecture's §2.5 + phase Depends-on graph. If §19's flat sequence is carried verbatim as the anchor, tasks-gen has no parallelization basis and will produce a single serial track by default.

dimension: D14 — PARALLELIZATION SEAMS / the §2.5 DAG (import-direction DAG, forced-serial spine vs independent tracks, Appendix-A contracts to freeze, single- vs multi-track)
summary: The Brain-1 draft does NOT contain the artifact D14 audits. The finalized ARCHITECTURE.md template mandates a "§2.5 — Subsystem dependency DAG & parallelization seams" section plus an "Appendix A — Model/contract inventory" (scaffold/templates/ARCHITECTURE.md:50,105), and §2.5 is explicitly where the import-direction DAG and parallel TRACKS are derived. ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md has neither: §6 lists the 16 subsystems with ZERO dependency edges, and §19 gives a 0–10 linear "build order" that conflates a serial build sequence with an import-direction DAG (they are not the same thing — a build order says what to build first; a DAG says what imports what, which is what determines parallel tracks). No node→node import graph, no statement of forced-serial spine vs independent tracks, no contract-freeze list, and no single-vs-multi-track decision exist anywhere in the planning set. This is the central D14 gap and it is load-bearing: /tasks-gen derives the Parallelization plan / Track map directly from §2.5, and CLAUDE.md's track-naming + per-track-worktree rules key off it. I derive the DAG below so the finalizer has a concrete starting point. Net: the architecture is a clean ports-and-adapters hexagon (D-21) that PERMITS healthy parallelization, but the spine (ports → manifest/registry → ingest → index) is forced-serial and several cross-cutting nodes (redactor, grounding, ports, providers) are shared contracts that MUST freeze before any track forks. Multi-track is correct and supported by D-21, but only AFTER the spine lands and the Appendix-A contracts freeze; a naive fork would collide on the chunk schema, the manifest schema (still OPEN per OQ-6), the HostPort interface, and the Anchor/ProvenancePacket types.

findings.finding: The §2.5 import-direction DAG does not exist in the draft. The finalized-ARCHITECTURE.md template REQUIRES a '§2.5 — Subsystem dependency DAG & parallelization seams' (scaffold/templates/ARCHITECTURE.md:50-64) with nodes=subsystems, edges='depends on/imports from', an explicit import-direction rule, the independent-subsystems list (the parallel-track seams), and the shared-contracts-to-freeze list. ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §6 lists the 16 subsystems (ingest/index/retrieval/grounding/federation/agent/drift/sessions/providers/ports/redactor/manifest+registry, plus cli/mcp/app/host-adapters/observability entrypoints) but draws ZERO edges between them. §19 gives a linear build order (0–10) which is NOT a DAG — it cannot answer 'which subsystems share no dependency path' (the seam question). The single deliverable D14 audits is therefore absent and must be authored at finalize.
findings.evidence: scaffold/templates/ARCHITECTURE.md:50-64 (mandated §2.5) vs docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:47-50 (§6, modules with no edges) and :89-90 (§19, linear build order)
findings.proposed_fix: Author §2.5 in the finalized ARCHITECTURE.md with the import-direction rule 'entrypoints(cli/mcp/app/host-adapters) → agent → {retrieval, drift, federation} → grounding → index → ingest → {redactor, providers, ports, manifest/registry}; ports/redactor depend on nothing; observability is a leaf sink everyone emits to; no upward or cross-sibling imports'. Include the Mermaid flowchart and the explicit seam + freeze lists (see D14-3/D14-4/D14-5).

findings.finding: The forced-serial SPINE is not identified, and it is non-trivial. Derived from the artifacts, the unavoidable serial chain is: ports (interfaces — DOMAIN_MODEL line 31-34) → manifest/registry + the LanceDB chunk schema (DATA_MODEL §1,③) → ingest (discover/classify/chunk/REDACT) → index (embed/LanceDB-write/optimize/version) → retrieval (hybrid/rerank/hydrate) → grounding gate → agent. Nothing downstream of 'embed one chunk into LanceDB with a live anchor' can be built or tested until that chain exists — this is exactly D-18's mandated first vertical slice (add→ingest→embed→retrieve→grounded answer→eval green). The draft never names this as the critical path, so /tasks-gen has no basis to distinguish spine tasks (which serialize) from breadth tasks (which fork).
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:59 (D-18 first vertical slice); docs/planning/DATA_MODEL.md:9-32 (chunk schema is the spine contract); docs/planning/DOMAIN_MODEL.md:38-44 (invariants 1-7 all sit on the index→anchor→grounding chain)
findings.proposed_fix: In §2.5, state explicitly: 'Forced-serial spine = ports → manifest/chunk-schema → ingest → index → retrieval → grounding → agent (the D-18 vertical slice). No track forks until the spine reaches a queryable+grounded one-repo index.' Mark these as Track-0/single-track in the Parallelization plan.

findings.finding: The independent (parallel-eligible) TRACKS are never enumerated. From the derived DAG, the subsystems that share no dependency path with each other (and so can fork AFTER the spine + frozen contracts) are: (T-A) federation router (reads N indexes read-only — depends on index's read interface + registry, NOT on agent/drift/sessions); (T-B) sync/freshness + drift radar (depends on index write + anchor revalidation, not on federation/mcp/app); (T-C) sessions/episode-cards (opt-in, depends on redactor + index + provider, isolated otherwise — DOMAIN_MODEL line 29); (T-D) MCP server entrypoint (depends on retrieval + redactor egress, parallel to the desktop app); (T-E) Tauri desktop app/UI (depends on the core's public API over loopback/stdio, fully parallel to T-A/T-B/T-C once that API freezes); (T-F) observability wiring (a leaf ObservabilitySink the whole system emits to — D-9, parallelizable last); (T-G) providers bake-offs (O-BAKEOFF, behind the EmbeddingProvider/Reranker ports — parallel from day one). The draft's §19 sequences these serially (steps 5-10) but they are NOT serially DEPENDENT; treating the build order as the dependency graph would needlessly under-parallelize.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:89-90 (§19 lists them as sequential steps 5-10); docs/planning/DOMAIN_MODEL.md:31-34 (port-isolated edges); docs/planning/USER_FLOWS.md:37-57 (F5/F6/F7/F8 are independent flows)
findings.proposed_fix: In §2.5 list T-A..T-G as the parallel-track seams and state which port/contract each forks behind. Note the build-order (§19) is a sequencing PREFERENCE, not a dependency constraint, for the post-spine subsystems.

findings.finding: The Appendix-A contract-freeze set is not derived, and several contracts that MUST freeze before tracks fork are still under-specified or OPEN. The template rule (scaffold/templates/ARCHITECTURE.md:64,107): any Appendix-A model whose § is crossed by a §2.5 edge is a cross-track shared contract and MUST freeze before tracks fork. The cross-track contracts I derive are: (1) the LanceDB CHUNK schema (DATA_MODEL §1 — crossed by ingest→index→retrieval→federation→sessions, i.e. nearly every track); (2) the .project-brain/manifest.json + global registry schema (crossed by ingest, federation, drift, app, mcp) — STILL OPEN per OQ-6 ('freeze at first ingest slice'); (3) the HostPort interface (crossed by every mutating path + both adapters — D-21); (4) the EmbeddingProvider/Reranker/ContextStrategy/ModelProvider port signatures (crossed by index, retrieval, sessions, the bake-off track); (5) Anchor (crossed by index, grounding, drift, retrieval); (6) ProvenancePacket + EvidenceRef (crossed by agent, mcp, app, and the NexusOps seam — must serialize 1:1 to NexusOps's 11-value EvidenceType/22 IdKind, MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md §3,§5); (7) the store-level version stamp {model,dim,schema,sha} (crossed by index, federation gating, drift). The draft has NO Appendix A, so none of these is flagged as a freeze-before-fork contract, and #2 is openly unfrozen.
findings.evidence: scaffold/templates/ARCHITECTURE.md:64,107 (freeze rule); docs/planning/DATA_MODEL.md:9-45,48-51 (chunk/manifest/anchor/provenance schemas, all 'rough-draft'); docs/planning/OPEN_QUESTIONS.md:12 (OQ-6 manifest schema OPEN); docs/integrations/MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md:45-55,78 (22 IdKind + 11 EvidenceType frozen on the platform side)
findings.proposed_fix: Build Appendix A in the finalized ARCHITECTURE.md listing contracts 1-7 with their § anchors, and add a bold freeze gate in §2.5: 'These 7 models freeze before any post-spine track forks; OQ-6 (manifest schema) MUST be resolved at the first ingest slice — it is a fork blocker for the federation/drift/app tracks.' Confirm the chunk/manifest schema freeze with the owner.

findings.finding: Two cross-cutting nodes break a naive layered DAG and must be modeled as shared dependencies, not as a single pipeline stage. (a) The REDACTOR is depended on at TWO points: index-time (redact-before-embed, FR-4/PH-2/T-1) AND MCP-egress (redact-at-boundary regardless of caller, FR-13/T-6) AND cloud-generation egress (D-15). So redactor is a leaf contract that ingest, mcp, AND agent/providers all import — a fork on the MCP track and the index track both depend on the SAME frozen redactor interface + corpus. (b) GROUNDING (anchor parse/revalidate/gate/provenance) is imported by retrieval, agent, drift, AND the federation answer path — it is a hub, not a downstream stage. The draft's §8 pipeline diagram presents both as linear pipeline stages, which hides that they are shared contracts crossing multiple tracks. If they are not frozen first, the MCP track and the drift track will both mutate the redactor/anchor contract mid-build (a cross-track Finding per team-protocol).
findings.evidence: docs/planning/THREAT_MODEL.md:13,18 (T-1 redact-before-embed + T-6 redact-at-MCP-boundary, same engine line 30); docs/planning/REQUIREMENTS.md:9,20 (FR-4 + FR-13); docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:55-57 (§8 presents redactor/grounding as linear stages)
findings.proposed_fix: In §2.5 model redactor and grounding as fan-in shared nodes (multiple inbound edges) and add both to the Appendix-A freeze set. State that the redaction property/fuzz corpus (SR-1) and the Anchor state machine must be frozen+green before the MCP and drift tracks fork.

findings.finding: The single-track-vs-multi-track decision is not made, and the planning set contains a contradiction the finalizer must resolve. Multi-track IS correct and architecturally supported: D-21's one-core/two-adapters hexagon with port isolation is precisely what enables parallel tracks behind frozen ports, and the independent flows F5/F6/F7/F8 + the desktop UI confirm real parallelism. BUT scaffold/templates/CLAUDE.md (single-operator fallback section) states: 'Parallel tracks + per-track worktrees are team-mode only — a single human bridging two sessions cannot drive N parallel worktree-teams; the Phase/Track DAG collapses to a serial build order.' The owner is a portfolio SOLO developer (PRODUCT_BRIEF persona A). So whether multi-track is ACTUALLY exercised depends on whether the build runs as an agent-team or single-operator — an unstated precondition. The DAG should be authored multi-track (it is the correct dependency truth and survives both modes), but §2.5 must state the seams are a parallelization plan IF team-mode, else a serial sequencing hint.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:67-68 (D-21 hexagon enables parallelism); scaffold/templates/CLAUDE.md (single-operator fallback: 'Parallel tracks ... are team-mode only ... DAG collapses to a serial build order'); docs/planning/PRODUCT_BRIEF.md:24 (owner = solo developer)
findings.proposed_fix: Author §2.5 as a multi-track DAG (correct dependency truth), and add one sentence: 'The seams below are parallel build TRACKS under agent-team mode; under single-operator they read as a serial sequencing hint (one track at a time, single working tree).' Flag the build-mode choice as a question-for-human.

findings.finding: Will the build run as an agent-team (multi-worktree, true parallel tracks) or single-operator (serial)? This determines whether §2.5's seams are an executable parallelization plan or a sequencing hint, and whether the per-track worktree/naming machinery in CLAUDE.md is even in play. The owner is a solo developer (persona A), which leans single-operator, but the production-grade/no-timebox posture and the agent-team scaffold suggest team-mode is intended. Unstated in every artifact.
findings.evidence: scaffold/templates/CLAUDE.md (team-mode vs single-operator fallback); docs/planning/PRODUCT_BRIEF.md:24 (solo owner); docs/planning/CONSTRAINTS.md:30 (no timebox)
findings.proposed_fix: Ask the owner: agent-team build (parallel worktree tracks) or single-operator (serial)? Record the answer in §2.5 so /tasks-gen emits the right Track map shape.

findings.finding: The NexusOpsHost track (T-P2) is correctly out-of-band but its fork-point should be stated in §2.5 for completeness. NexusOpsHost is propose-only, deferred (P2), and depends only on the frozen HostPort + the published nexus-brain-core public API + the NexusOps seam contracts (MAIN_PLATFORM_INTERFACE.md). It is a fully independent late track that forks behind exactly two frozen contracts (HostPort + the core public API) and never touches the spine. Worth naming so the seam's freeze requirement (IR-1 contract-snapshot test) is visibly tied to the DAG.
findings.evidence: docs/planning/ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:68-69,92-93 (§12/§20); docs/planning/DECISIONS.md:67-68 (D-21 additive adapter); docs/planning/RISKS.md:23 (IR-1)
findings.proposed_fix: Add NexusOpsHost as a dashed P2 track in the §2.5 graph with the note 'forks behind frozen HostPort + published core API; never touches the spine.'

summary: The draft names every hot path the PRD implies but assigns a numeric budget to NONE of them. The only performance row in the whole artifact set (EVALUATION_CRITERIA §Metrics "Perf") is the LanceDB optimize()/RAM/disk axis, and even that defers all thresholds to a future bake-off ("within budget (O-LANCE-BAKEOFF)", "thresholds set at first bake-off"). Query latency, index lag after save, drift-detection latency, time-to-first-grounded-answer, federation fan-out latency, re-embed cost, and local-model inference are each named as axes (in PRD §15, NFR-1/NFR-3, A-10, RISKS TR-2/TR-5) but carry zero target numbers. For a PRODUCTION-GRADE, no-timebox posture where observability is baseline, the absence of any latency SLO/budget — even a coarse p50/p95 for the interactive ask path — is the central gap. Three of the named axes (index lag, drift latency, time-to-first-answer) are ALSO declared PRD Success Metrics, so shipping GA without thresholds means a "success metric" that cannot pass/fail. None of these absences is marked a deliberate deferral with a rationale; they are simply unstated. Per instructions I treat unstated budgets as question-for-human (never invent a number), and flag the highest-leverage ones (interactive query latency, index lag) as load-bearing because a GA gate decision rides on them. The maintenance-contract perf axis IS at least acknowledged and routed to a spike (O-LANCE-BAKEOFF), which is the correct posture — but its sibling hot paths got no equivalent treatment.

findings.finding: No query/answer latency budget exists anywhere for the primary interactive hot path (the `ask` / drawer query). The retrieval pipeline is fully specified structurally — hybrid → rerank (~30–50→~10) → CodeGraph tools → whole-file hydration → frontier-model generation + Citations API → grounding gate (ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT §8) — but no end-to-end p50/p95 target is stated for either the classic single-pass lookup path or the agentic-RAG multi-hop path. The research dossier itself flags this is latency-sensitive ('1M-token requests are slower than fetching 5 chunks — prefer retrieval when latency-sensitive even if it'd fit', RESEARCH_DOSSIER §84) and that agentic RAG costs '3–10× more tokens' (§83), yet NFR-3 only encodes the structural routing rule, not a latency bound. STAKEHOLDERS.md:11 lists 'too slow to be daily-driver' as the owner's explicit anti-goal, so a budget is a stated product requirement with no number attached.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §8 (Query time pipeline); REQUIREMENTS.md NFR-3; docs/research/RESEARCH_DOSSIER.md §83-84; STAKEHOLDERS.md:11
findings.proposed_fix: Owner to set, before GA, a p50/p95 interactive-query budget split by path: (a) classic single-pass lookup (cached long-context, <~200K corpus) and (b) agentic-RAG multi-hop. Add as an NFR row + an EVALUATION_CRITERIA Perf metric with a hard/soft gate. If the owner chooses to defer, mark it explicitly as a deliberate deferral with the GA risk acknowledged. Do not invent the number here.

findings.finding: Three named PRD Success Metrics have NO threshold, making them unmeasurable as pass/fail gates: 'Index lag after file save', 'Drift detection latency', and 'Time to first grounded answer after project add' (PRD §15 Freshness). The draft restates these only as bounded-not-numbered: NFR-1 says 'Index lag bounded after file save' and EVALUATION_CRITERIA §Metrics Freshness row says 'bounded; converges' with no value. A success metric without a target cannot defend itself at GA.
findings.evidence: PRD.md §15 (Success Metrics → Freshness); REQUIREMENTS.md NFR-1; EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md §Metrics (Freshness row 'bounded; converges')
findings.proposed_fix: Owner to assign a concrete budget to each of the three Freshness success metrics (e.g. watcher-driven index lag p95, drift-radar revalidation latency, cold time-to-first-grounded-answer after `add`). Wire each into the eval harness as a measured number with a regression direction. Flag as question-for-human if the owner has no basis yet — but a Success Metric must not ship thresholdless.

findings.finding: Local-model inference performance is an unverified ASSUMPTION, not a budget. A-10 states qwen3-embedding-4b (~2.5GB) is 'fast enough on target Macs for interactive re-index + query' but tags it '[ASSUMPTION] ... must verify' with no spike owning the verification and no throughput/latency target (e.g. tokens/s for embed, ms for rerank of ~30–50 candidates). This is a hot path on every local-default query and every re-embed; the cloud opt-in is named as the escape hatch but the threshold that triggers 'you need cloud' is undefined.
findings.evidence: ASSUMPTIONS.md A-10; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §13 (local qwen3 default); EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md §Bake-offs (no local-inference latency bake-off listed — only reranker quality and LanceDB maintenance bake-offs)
findings.proposed_fix: Promote A-10 to a named spike/bake-off (sibling to O-LANCE-BAKEOFF) that measures local embed throughput + rerank latency on the target Mac baseline, and have the owner set the interactive-acceptable budget that defines the local→cloud fallback trigger. Name the reference hardware (see D15-7).

findings.finding: Federation fan-out latency has no budget. §9 / DIAGRAM_PLAN describe Router → N×{LanceDB, CodeGraph} read-only → RRF union with on-demand worker activation + LRU idle eviction, and §18 mentions 'Worker evicted → cheap re-attach' and CodeGraph '300s idle normal' — but there is no target for portfolio-query latency as N scales, no cold-worker-reattach budget, and no fan-out concurrency/timeout bound. RISKS TR-2 covers maintenance-contract stutter but not fan-out latency under a real multi-repo portfolio. For a portfolio-first product (the standalone north star), cross-repo query latency at N repos is a core UX axis.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §9, §18; DIAGRAM_PLAN.md (fan-out diagram); RISKS.md TR-2 (covers RAM/optimize stutter, not fan-out latency)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a federation-latency budget parameterized by N (e.g. p95 portfolio query at the expected portfolio size) plus a cold-worker re-attach budget and a per-worker fan-out timeout, so degraded/slow repos can be dropped-and-marked rather than blocking the whole answer. Owner to set N and the targets.

findings.finding: Re-embed / blue-green generation cost has no budget. TR-5 names 'Embedding drift / re-embed cost on model/dim change across a portfolio' as a Med/Med risk with mitigation 'content-hash resume; re-embed only deltas' (RISKS.md), and PH-5/D-14 mandate blue-green re-embed on model/dim change — but there is no wall-clock or RAM budget for a full-portfolio re-embed, nor a 'how long is a model swap allowed to take' target. This is the operability cost of D-23 provider-switching ('switching a provider = blue-green re-embed', §13), which the product treats as a routine action.
findings.evidence: RISKS.md TR-5; REQUIREMENTS.md PH-5; ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §13, §7 ('Blue-green re-embed on model/dim change'); DECISIONS D-14
findings.proposed_fix: Owner to set a re-embed budget (per-repo and full-portfolio) covering wall-clock and peak RAM, since provider-switching is a first-class action; tie it to the RAM-bounded batched-build limit so re-embed is bounded by design, not incident-driven.

findings.finding: The one performance budget that IS acknowledged — LanceDB optimize() latency, index-build RAM, steady-state disk — still carries no numbers; it is correctly routed to a spike (O-LANCE-BAKEOFF) but with 'within budget' and 'thresholds set at first bake-off' as the only specification. This is the BEST-handled hot path (it has an owner spike, a risk row TR-2, and an A-4 assumption), so the posture is right; the gap is only that the budget values remain TBD until the bake-off runs. This is the model the other hot paths (D15-1..D15-5) should follow.
findings.evidence: EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md §Metrics (Perf row: 'within budget (O-LANCE-BAKEOFF)') and §Bake-offs; RISKS.md TR-2; ASSUMPTIONS.md A-4; DECISIONS D-25 W1/W4
findings.proposed_fix: Keep the O-LANCE-BAKEOFF routing but require the bake-off to emit concrete numbers (optimize() p95, peak build RAM ceiling, steady-state disk/repo) that become GA gate values, not just a qualitative 'invisible to users' pass. Mirror this pattern for the other hot paths.

findings.finding: There is no named reference hardware / baseline machine against which any performance budget would be measured. The docs say 'a typical Mac' (A-4), 'target Macs' (A-10), 'Apple Silicon arm64' (CONSTRAINTS C-1) — but no specific baseline (e.g. M-series tier + RAM). Every latency/throughput/RAM budget proposed above is meaningless without a fixed reference machine, and 'typical Mac' spans an order of magnitude in memory bandwidth and unified-RAM ceiling, which directly governs local-inference speed (D15-3) and RAM-bounded build batching (PH-1).
findings.evidence: ASSUMPTIONS.md A-4, A-10; CONSTRAINTS.md C-1; EVALUATION_CRITERIA.md §Bake-offs ('a typical Mac')
findings.proposed_fix: Owner to pin a reference-hardware baseline (chip tier + unified RAM) that all performance budgets and bake-offs are measured against, plus a stated minimum-supported spec. Without this, every budget number is unanchored.

findings.finding: The grounding gate adds a mandatory post-generation validation pass on EVERY cited answer (post-validate every cited span exists; FR-9, §8) and anchor revalidation runs 'continuously' (FR-10). Neither has a latency budget, and the grounding gate sits on the critical interactive path between generation and answer-display. It is unclear whether revalidation is synchronous (adds to query latency) or background (adds index lag) — the draft does not say, so its contribution to either D15-1 (query latency) or D15-2 (index lag) cannot be bounded.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md §8 (grounding gate in query path), §10 (drift radar revalidates anchors); REQUIREMENTS.md FR-9, FR-10
findings.proposed_fix: Owner/architect to specify whether grounding-gate span-validation and anchor revalidation are on the synchronous answer path or background, and budget each accordingly. If synchronous, its cost must be inside the D15-1 query budget; if background, inside the D15-2 freshness budget.

summary: The draft and its planning artifacts are deep on the trust/grounding/redaction/maintenance-contract spine but miss several WHOLE categories that production-grade scope (no timebox) makes baseline, not deferrable. The most load-bearing gaps: (1) cloud-API cost/budget control and rate-limit handling — the only "cost" mentions are re-embed cost and routing latency; nothing bounds Claude/Voyage spend, token budgets, or 429/quota backoff, which is a real operability and trust-of-the-wallet concern for an agentic RAG loop that fans out queries; (2) concurrency between the desktop-UI embedded agent and external MCP agents hitting the SAME project store — the single-writer-per-index law covers index workers, but not a UI-triggered sync/re-index/owned-doc-refresh racing a concurrent MCP query, nor two mutation paths (embedded agent + external agent) both invoking owned-doc refresh; (3) cloud model deprecation/sunset handling (a retired embedding or generation model is distinct from a user-initiated provider swap and has no flow); (4) user-facing data export/backup/portability and disaster recovery (uninstall purges, but there's no backup/restore/export of the portfolio store/registry beyond "rebuild from source"); (5) cold-start / truly-empty-portfolio first-run UX (R-PARTIAL only covers a thin single repo, not zero projects); (6) telemetry-CONSENT UX (off-by-default is decided, but no consent/disclosure surface is specified); (7) accessibility/a11y of the Tauri desktop UI (zero coverage — keyboard nav, screen reader, WCAG); (8) licensing of INDEXED third-party/vendored code (C-11 covers shipped-component licenses only — nothing about a user indexing GPL/proprietary third-party code and sending it to cloud embeddings); (9) i18n / non-English code+docs (zero coverage; redaction entropy heuristics and chunking assume English-ish text). Items 1, 2, 3, 4 are load-bearing (they change contracts/decisions the owner must confirm); 5–9 are important-to-nice gaps for a polished production product.

findings.finding: No cloud-API cost/budget control or rate-limit/quota handling category. The agentic-RAG loop fans out `search` queries, hydrates whole files, reranks, and calls Claude per answer (plus cloud embeddings on opt-in), yet nothing bounds token spend, sets a per-session/per-day budget, surfaces estimated cost, or handles 429/quota/backoff from Claude or Voyage. The only 'cost' references are 're-embed cost' (TR-5) and 'validate on real cost/latency' for the ~200K routing threshold (OQ-5) — neither is a spend-control mechanism. For a production-grade tool whose default posture sends generation (and optionally embeddings) to paid cloud APIs, unbounded/un-observable spend and unhandled rate limits are an operability and degradation-path gap.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§8 (query-time fan-out + frontier model per answer); DECISIONS.md:D-13 (agentic RAG)/D-23 (cloud generation default hybrid posture); RISKS.md:TR-5 (only 're-embed cost'); OPEN_QUESTIONS.md:OQ-5; REQUIREMENTS.md (no cost/rate-limit NFR/PH row); PRD.md:§4 G3 mentions privacy but not cost
findings.proposed_fix: Add a cost/quota category to ARCHITECTURE/REQUIREMENTS: (a) a token/cost budget per query and per session with a configurable ceiling and a degrade-to-retrieval-only or refuse path on breach; (b) rate-limit/429/quota-exhaustion handling for Claude + Voyage adapters with exponential backoff and a user-visible 'cloud throttled, degraded' state (parallels PH-9 graceful degradation); (c) surface estimated/actual spend in observability and optionally the UI. Confirm with owner whether a hard budget cap is in-scope for MVP.

findings.finding: Concurrency between the desktop-UI embedded agent and external MCP agents on the SAME project store is unaddressed beyond index writers. Architectural law §4.2.5 establishes single-writer-per-index + read-only federation router, but the realistic standalone scenario is: the user's desktop UI (embedded agent) triggers a sync / blue-green re-index / owned-doc refresh on project P while an external MCP agent (Claude Code/Codex/CI) is concurrently querying P — and, separately, both the embedded agent AND an external agent could each invoke owned-doc refresh or `sync` on P. There is no coordination/locking/serialization contract for (a) read-during-reindex (atomic-swap is mentioned for crash, not for live concurrent readers), (b) two independent callers racing a mutation in the bounded allowlist, or (c) a single per-project worker serving two surfaces at once. D-3 explicitly ships BOTH surfaces over one core, so this is a day-one concern.
findings.evidence: ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§4.2 law 5 (single-writer/read-only router only); DECISIONS.md:D-3 (two surfaces, one core)/D-25 W3 (single-writer addresses optimistic-commit conflicts, not multi-caller mutation serialization); USERS.md:Non-Human Actors (embedded agent + external agents both clients of the core; one per-project worker); USER_FLOWS.md:F6/F8/F9 (sync/refresh/re-embed) with no concurrent-reader/concurrent-caller contract
findings.proposed_fix: Add a concurrency-and-coordination section: per-project worker serializes mutating operations (sync/re-index/owned-doc refresh) via a queue or lock; readers always see a consistent generation (atomic-swap guarantees applied to live concurrent queries, not just crash); define behavior when a second caller requests a mutation already in flight (coalesce/queue/reject-with-marker). Owner must confirm the locking model (single in-process worker vs advisory file lock across UI + headless CLI processes that may both touch ~/.project-brain).

findings.finding: Cloud model DEPRECATION/sunset is an unhandled category, distinct from the user-initiated provider swap that blue-green covers. D-14/D-23/PH-5 handle the user CHOOSING a new model (blue-green re-embed). But a vendor retiring an embedding model (Voyage) or a generation model (Claude) — returning errors or silently substituting — is different: an index stamped to a now-dead embedding model can't be incrementally re-embedded with the same model, and a deprecated generation model breaks `ask`. No detection, no forced-migration flow, no 'your index was built on a sunset model' user signal.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md:D-14 (blue-green on user model change)/D-23 (provider menu)/PH-5; DATA_MODEL.md:store-level stamp pins embedding_model+dimension; no flow in USER_FLOWS.md for vendor-side model retirement; ASSUMPTIONS.md A-2 marks current-best models VERIFIED but assumes persistence
findings.proposed_fix: Add a model-deprecation flow: provider adapters detect model-unavailable/deprecation responses and surface a 'this index is pinned to a retired model — re-embed required' state; provide a forced-migration path (blue-green to a current model) and degrade `ask` to retrieval-only or an alternate generation model if the pinned one is gone. Add an assumption/risk row for vendor model churn.

findings.finding: No user-facing data export / backup / restore / portability category. The Lance format is praised as 'open + portable' for vendor-lock-in (D-25), and recovery is 'rebuild from source' (T-11/Flow 10), and uninstall PURGES ~/.project-brain (Flow 12). But there is no backup/restore of the portfolio (registry + per-project stores + episode cards + policy.yaml + keychain refs), no export of the user's accumulated memory (episode cards and provenance are NOT reproducible from source — they're derived from transcripts that may be gone), and no migration-to-a-new-machine story. For a tool whose whole value is accumulated project memory, losing it on disk failure or machine migration is a real production gap.
findings.evidence: DATA_MODEL.md:②③ (episode cards Brain-owned, derived from transcripts; registry global); USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 12 (uninstall purges, only 'preserve local data' toggle)/Flow 10 (recovery = rebuild from source, but episode cards aren't source-reproducible); DECISIONS.md:D-25 (portable format cited only for anti-lock-in)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a backup/export/restore category: a `nexus export`/`import` (or backup) covering the registry, per-project Lance datasets, episode cards, manifests, and policy (keychain refs handled per-OS), plus a documented machine-migration path. Explicitly note which assets are source-reproducible vs irreplaceable (episode cards, commit-link confidence) so the irreplaceable ones are backup-prioritized.

findings.finding: Cold-start / truly-empty-portfolio first-run UX is missing. R-PARTIAL handles a thin/incomplete SINGLE repo, and `setup`/`add` flows assume the user proceeds to add a repo. But the first-launch state of the desktop UI with ZERO projects, no models pulled, and no API keys — the empty-state that decides whether a new user succeeds or bounces — has no flow or UX requirement. Given install friction is already flagged (PR-3: CodeGraph + Ollama + multi-GB pulls), the empty-portfolio onboarding is the highest-leverage UX moment and is unspecified.
findings.evidence: USER_FLOWS.md:Flow 1 setup/Flow 2 add (no empty-portfolio UI state); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§11 (Desktop UI = chat + evidence chips + project mgmt, no empty/first-run state); RISKS.md:PR-3 (install friction acknowledged but mitigation is 'progress UX' only)
findings.proposed_fix: Add a cold-start UX requirement: define the zero-project desktop first-run (guided add-first-repo, model-pull progress, API-key/privacy-posture choice as onboarding, what `ask` shows before any project exists). Tie it to PR-3's install-friction mitigation as a measurable onboarding success path.

findings.finding: Telemetry-CONSENT UX is unspecified even though the policy is decided. D-22/T-7 lock 'OTel off by default, local-only, never phone home,' which is the correct posture — but there is no consent/disclosure surface: how the user is told instrumentation exists, how the opt-in 'diagnostics/local tracing' toggle is presented, and what guarantees are shown when they enable it (that it points only at their own local collector). For a privacy-first product this is part of the trust story, not just an engineering default.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md:D-22 (off-by-default opt-in toggle described as a capability, not a UX); THREAT_MODEL.md:T-7; REQUIREMENTS.md:PH-6 (instrumented-but-silent); no consent-UX flow in USER_FLOWS.md
findings.proposed_fix: Add a telemetry-consent UX requirement: a clear in-product disclosure of the off-by-default local-only instrumentation, an explicit opt-in toggle with a plain-language guarantee (local collector only, no egress), and reference it from the privacy posture chosen at setup (Flow 1 step 5).

findings.finding: Accessibility (a11y) of the Tauri desktop UI is entirely absent. The product ships a real desktop app (D-17) with chat, evidence chips, freshness banners, and project management, but no planning artifact mentions keyboard navigation, screen-reader/ARIA support, focus management, color-contrast of the freshness/confidence markers, or WCAG conformance. Production-grade scope for a shipped GUI normally includes a baseline a11y bar; reusing NexusOps-ui-kit doesn't guarantee it.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md:D-17 (Tauri desktop app + NexusOps-ui-kit); ARCHITECTURE_DRAFT.md:§11 (Desktop UI features listed, no a11y); grep across docs/planning: zero hits for accessibility/a11y/WCAG/keyboard/screen-reader/contrast
findings.proposed_fix: Add an accessibility requirement (NFR or PH): a baseline a11y target for the desktop UI (keyboard-navigable chat + evidence chips, screen-reader labels, sufficient contrast for freshness/confidence/staleness indicators which are trust-critical and must not rely on color alone). Confirm whether NexusOps-ui-kit already supplies this or it must be added.

findings.finding: Licensing of INDEXED third-party/vendored code is unaddressed; C-11 only governs SHIPPED components. The product indexes 'any repo' (G5) which routinely contains vendored/third-party code with restrictive licenses, and the default hybrid posture sends content to cloud generation (and optionally cloud embeddings). Sending GPL/proprietary/confidential third-party code in a repo to a cloud API is a legal/compliance category with no mention — distinct from the secret/PII redaction story (that's about secrets, not copyright/licensing). `.brainignore` exists but there is no guidance or default for excluding vendored/license-restricted code from cloud egress.
findings.evidence: PRD.md:§4 G5 (works on any repo)/§NG; CONSTRAINTS.md:C-11 (licenses of SHIPPED components only); DECISIONS.md:D-23 (cloud generation default; voyage opt-in); FR-2 honors .gitignore/.brainignore but no license-aware exclusion; THREAT_MODEL.md scope covers secrets, not copyright
findings.proposed_fix: Add a note/requirement distinguishing indexed-code licensing from secret redaction: at minimum document that cloud egress sends repo content (so users with license-restricted/third-party code should choose local-only posture or .brainignore vendored dirs), and consider a default to exclude common vendored paths (node_modules/vendor/third_party) from cloud-bound paths. Flag as a question-for-human whether license-awareness is in MVP scope.

findings.finding: i18n / non-English code and documentation is an unconsidered category. The redaction engine uses Shannon-entropy heuristics on KEY=value/JSON lines and English-ish token prefixes; chunking uses heading-split and AST parsing; embedding models and rerankers are chosen on code-RAG benchmarks. None of the artifacts consider non-English doc text, non-ASCII identifiers, or RTL content — which can affect entropy-based redaction false positives/negatives, chunk-boundary heuristics, and answer quality. Likely low priority for the solo-dev persona but unstated.
findings.evidence: THREAT_MODEL.md:Redaction engine (entropy + prefix heuristics, English-oriented); DATA_MODEL.md:chunk model (no locale/encoding field); grep across docs/planning: zero hits for i18n/locale/non-english/unicode
findings.proposed_fix: Add a one-line scope note: state whether non-English docs/code are in or out of scope for MVP; if in, verify the redaction entropy thresholds and chunking heuristics on non-ASCII/non-English corpora. At minimum record it as an explicit non-goal so it's a decision, not an omission.

findings.finding: Disk-space / storage-budget governance for the portfolio is implicit but never bounded. D-25 W2 cleans up old Lance versions and per-project datasets keep builds small, but there is no portfolio-level disk budget, no eviction/archival of cold projects' on-disk indexes (only in-memory worker LRU eviction is specified), and no user-visible 'your brain is using N GB' signal. Across many repos with blue-green generations and tagged versions (GC-exempt), steady-state disk could grow unbounded for the solo-dev-with-many-repos primary persona.
findings.evidence: DECISIONS.md:D-25 W2 (cleanup_old_versions, tagged versions GC-exempt)/A-4 (steady-state disk to be verified in O-LANCE-BAKEOFF); USERS.md (worker idle-EVICTION is memory-only, LRU); no disk-budget or cold-store-archival requirement in REQUIREMENTS.md
findings.proposed_fix: Confirm with owner: is there a portfolio disk budget or cold-project on-disk archival/eviction in MVP scope, plus a user-visible storage signal? If GC-exempt git-SHA tags accumulate across many repos, define a tag-retention policy so tagged versions don't defeat cleanup over time.
